We are grateful for the\ngrowing populanty of Objective English, and thank the readers for using it for their examination\nPreparation. New and revised edinons of this book which come at short intervals keep it updated,\nexam-onented, and suited to the current requirement of the students. A revised edition also helps us\nin providing our readers with new concepts and techniques to equip them even better to tackle the\nvarious questions in their competitive examinations.\n\nWho can Use this Book?\n\nThis book 1s aimed at aspirants of various jobs in banks, defence and insurance sector, government\noffices, private companies, embassies, ministries, non-Governmental organisations, and public\nundertakings. This book will also help those aspirants who compete for admission to prestigious\nprofessional and vocational institutes.\n\nCoverage of the Book\nThis book starts by giving general information about different examinations and then organises the\ntext in the following ways\n* Section A provides reading materials, concepts, techniques\ndrilling exercises\n\nups, figures, illustrations, and\n\n* Section B contains practice test papers compnising the latest types of questions set in various\nexaminations.\n\n* Section C contains more than forty previous years’ papers of various examinations.\n\nMain Features of the Book\n\nThis book has the unique distinction of being the first text designed to help students in solving English\nlanguage questions in an objective format. It imparts in a planned, systematic, and comprehensive way,\nfully solve the English questions appearing\n\nthe knowledge, concepts, and techniques required to succe:\nin various Competitive examinations,\n\nUnlike other books in the market which either deal with one or two types of questions or are purely\ngrammar-based, this book approaches the objective English of examination papers in a comprehensive\nmanner in the following ways\n\n+ It has been prepared after a careful scrutiny and analyses of the syllabi and questions of\ndifferent competitive examinations and the basic principles behind such tests.\n\n* In order to tackle objective English questions of competitive examinations, a candidate\nrequires a sound understanding of comprehension, applied grammar, vocabulary in usage,\nand logical application of language. These topics are thoroughly covered in this book\n\n6 | Objective English for Competitive Examinations\n\n* This book also covers secondary units like phrases, substitution, preposition, voice, and analogy\nwhich will help the candidates enhance their understanding of English language\n¢ Each chapter has dniling exercise sets at the end to help the students master their learning.\n\nHighlights of this Edition\n\nExhaustive changes and additions have been incorporated tn the fifth edition of this book keeping\nin view the changing trends of the examinations. Section A which comprises the staple of the book,\nte. the basic umits, has been retained and revised. The chapter on Vor in this section has been made\nmore comprehensive. Two new chapters on Spelling and Narration have been added. All concepts and\ntechniques have been maintained and improved wherever necessary.\nSections B and C have been extensively revised in the following ways\n\n* Completely fresh practice test papers prepared on the latest pattern of various examinations\nhave been added in Section B.\nSection C has been thoroughly revamped. This section has five units now and each contains\nquestions of previous years up to 2014 . The answers have also been provided along with\n\nthe analysis, trends, and vanations tn question types. This section also includes suggestions\nto help the students gain confidence, surety, and swiftness in dealing with the questions.\nComments included in this section will help students in designing the strategy required to\ntackle the questions in the examination. About 40 examination papers from sectors like\n\nBanking, Insurance, Defence, Business Management etc., have been included in this section\n\nWe hope this book in tts pres\n\nent form and format will be immensely useful to the aspiring\ncandidates. We also acknowledge all those who have extended cooperation in the preparation of this\nnew edition\n\nHari Monan Prasad\nUna Rant Siva\n\nPREFACE TO THE First EDITION\n\nT have been wating research papers and books of explanatory nature on literature, which were meant\nonly for a select audience. With this book, I hope to reach a much wider section of the student\ncommunity and others, trying relentlessly to compete for various jobs in Government and Public\nsectors.\n\nObjective English forms a part of the syllabi of different competitive examinations. It 1s basically\na string of three units: Grammar, Comprehension, Vocabulary. There ts certainly a dearth of good\nand reliable books, which completely cover all three units. A majority of the books available are mere\ncompilation of questions asked over the years. Keeping this in mind, I have tned to explain the concepts\nbehind every possible kind of test and have also endeavoured to develop simple techniques possible. In\naddition to these, practice sets at the end of every unit and 30 model test papers have been provided,\nwith a view to helping the student develop competence and confidence in tackling communication and\nlanguage tests. Consequently, the book has become both comprehensive and innovative.\n\nWhile preparing this book, my sons Rajmish and Soujanya, my daughter Swetali, my nephew\nBablu came out with various magazines, questions and self-developed problems, which helped me\nin the formulation of techniques. I am grateful to them for their keen interest in my book. I am also\nthankful to Dr Satguru Prasad my frend, who joyfully shared the painful task of proof-reading with me\nI shall be remiss, if I fail to mention my wife Ram, who extended her help in various invisible ways\nI sincerely hope the book will meet a good response. I shall greatly appreciate suggestions for further\nimprovement of the book\n\nHari Monan Prasad\nUna Rant Stvna\n\nul Buueaddy aie no,\nuoneulmex9 ay) jo\n\nsaadeg ay) ure8e\n\nsuoneniasqgo aqjos “Ayjeury\n\nje jeu\nSame sade qy8ry 129 Bulpueyssapun, Poa aa\npeay Ajjnyase> Iv aajos NO, IIL. waday ANOA W89L\n\nsyuauigas aun 7\nsaaydey)\n\nsiskjeuy rie duit, paquosaig stag aonzeld pia\npuoi pue ou ttede u LINHA BAlOs leuon!ppy pue A109\nAduanbasy ay, pes ho d $13S a9NIe1d\n\n3sa}e] ie saadeg\n\naonoeid OT\n\nCm SD\n\nSuluseay INO, asiwiydO\n\na yoog SIy} asn 0} MOH e\n\n©) suiexg asay} 50 yoeg 103 saidol [IV\n(sdiy pue su.ia}jeg) spieyaq SuIAry AOJ SJBS 9D1QIeId\nuononpo.yuy A1oyeuerldxy =\n\nPree SROFrULEED 6 UF 1DWDA TARE OK] Bd He DORR? wOIT Bey SRURY OL\n\n$ © , $ 5 ‘SHIENORS\n\n(2007 T1111) SNOILVNIWYX3 SNOIYVA NI G3NSV SNOILSIND JO SISATVNV ADN3NDIYI\n\nPRD, ROPE og 4 tpwNS 1HeFN OK] wd 88 ARETE? weed Oey SqUR OU)\n\n’ ?\n\ns $\n\n?\n\no\n\nHO Ste\n\n7 ovevecan\n\nze)\n\n7 poxotin\n\n30\n\né ?\n\nTworvousaan\n\nTOUNS\n\nINVLSISS¥ HL\n\nLcn¥ Os\n\n(2007 1111) SNOILVNIWYX3 SNOIYVA NI G3NSV SNOILSIND JO SISATVNV ADN3NDIYI\n\n‘UD PRURLAETD OF] UI SUNS |209E] OY; Ld 8 POFDED? Some of them have separate\nsections for comprehension where candidates have to\nwork on several passages. Hence, it 1s important that\nduc attention is paid to comprehension exercises\n\n[concert Summany |\n\n‘The word ‘comprehend’ means ‘to understand’. You\nare required to go through a passage, grasp its gist,\nand then answer the questions based on it. It involves\nthe use of your vocabulary, your sense of language,\nyour ability to locate meanings, your intelligence to\nassess the real spint of the passage, and your capability\ninfer conclusions from the given content. You should\nremember that your answers must be based on and\n\nnot by any outside source. Only in certain cases, the\ninference may take you outside the boundary of the\npassage but even then your answer must be a logical\nconsequence of the information in the passage\n\nIt ts advisable to improve your sense of language\nand stock of words by reading regularly and bringing\nnew words in your day-to-day usage of language.\nThough regular practice helps, while adopting the IFI\n(Information-Flimination-Inference) formula, the\nfollowing tips may prove useful.\n\nFormula\n\n‘The words—tnformation, elimination, and inference—may\nbe used as the main keys.\n\nconfirmed by the information given in the passage and Information\n| — !\nI | ]\nInformation Elimination Inference\nDirect Implied Simple Complex\n\nA.B | Object\n\nEvery passage offers you some ‘information’\nSome questions are straightforward and they may be\nanswered from the information within the passage\nitself. In the two passages taken up for illustration\nbelow, refer to Questons 3 and 5 in Passage | and\nQuestions 1, 2,4, 5, and 6in Passage 2. These questions\ncan be answered on the basis of the information given\nin the passage\n\n‘The information in the passage may be direct or\nimplied. Kor example, in Passage 1, Question 5 is based\non direct information, and Question 3 on implied\ninformaton.\n\nElimination\n\nThe process of ‘elimination’ entails saying no to\nalternatives on justifiable grounds until you have a\npositive answer Question 9 in Passage 2 1s one such\nexample Usually, this process helps in answering\nquestions of the “true or false” category\n\nInference\n\nThe word ‘inference’ means ‘to arnve at a logical\nconclusion’. Some questions which cannot be\nanswered by information or elimination require\nthe process of inference. Refer to Questions 3 and\n7 in Passage 2\n\nThere are umes when you may have tocom-bine\ntwo of these keys together. For example, see Question\n4in Passage 1. A and D can be eliminated in the first\nreading, B and C appear to be correct, but, if you\ncombine ‘elimination’ with ‘inference’, you find the\nclue in the desenption that the lady takes a whole day\nin wating the postcard. Thus, B 1s eliminated and C\nbecomes the correct choice.\n\nInference may be either simple or complex\nFor example, Question 1 requires simple inference. A\nstatementis made nght in the beginning of the passage\nand the storyof the ladyillustrates the factin the statement\nthat whatever time ts available for work, people tend to\nuse up. But Question 2 requires complex inference. The\nperson likely to take more time to do some work 1s: (2)\na busy man (b) a man of leisure. Though it seems that\n{a) 1s the answer, the logical outcome of the contents\nin the passage points towards (b). The answer is a man\nof leisure. Complex inference therfore implies\nreasoning\n\naminations\n\nStep3\n\nUnderline answers for questions\nFetained\n\nStep 4\n\nSolve other questions by IEI\nFormula\n\nFig 1.2 Time-saver IEI Formula\n\n‘Thus it is now evident that different questions of\na comprehension passage can be answered by any of\nthe three clues separately and sometimes by combining\nthem.\n\n[Furher Tips\n\nThe IFI formula helps every candidate. However,\nthose who have a sharp memory and can retain\ncertain facts on the first reading may work a little\ndifferently to save further time. Instead of reading the\npassage first, you read the questions and try to retain\nsome of them in your memory. Then while reading the\npassage you will be able to underline the answer and\nlater tick the correct choice in a flash\n\n(A) By IEI Formula\nM@ Passage 1\n\nRead the following passage and answer the\nquestions based on it.\n\nWork expands so as to fill the nme available for\nits completion. This is the principle\nof weight training Strands of muscle fibres become\nthicker and stronger in response to the demands\nplaced on them.\n\nOne of the great ments of weight training 1s\nthe strength of your heart. During weight traning, your\nheart is forced to beat faster and stronger in order to\npump sufficient blood to the muscles being worked. In\nume, your heart, like your body, will adapt to this extra\nworkload by becoming stronger and more efficient\nSince your body needs a given amount of blood to\nperform its daily tasks your heart will now need fewer\nbeats to pump the same quanuty of blood. Sounds\ngood? There's more. Your entire circulatory system\n1s given a thorough workout everytime you exercise,\nwhich increases its overall efficiency. Even the neural\npaths from your brain’s command centres to each\nindividual muscle become more effective, enabling\neasier recruitment of muscle fibres for carrying out\nphysical tasks. In essence, your body becomes a well:\noiled and finely-tuned piece of machinery, whirring\nalong without any break-down. In today’s stress filled\nworld, you need all help you can get\n\n1, What is the pnncipal training of weight\nlifting?\n\n6.\n\n(a) Adapting the body to muscle force\n\n(b) Adapting muscles to force implied on\nthem\n\n(c) Disposing extra-workload\n\n(d) Mechanised\nconditions\n\nWhat affects the nature of muscle fibres?\n\n(a) Intensity of workload\n\n(b) Alimentary system\n\n(c) Nutrition\n\n(d) Stress imposed on them\n\nresponse to external\n\n. This unfinished part of his experiment was\nperhaps even more difficult to achieve than the\nachievement of political freedom. Pohtical struggle\ninvolved fight against a foreign power and all one\ncould do was either join it or wish it success and give\nithis moral support. In establishing the social order of\nthis pattern, there was a lively possibility of a conflict\nansing between groups and classes of our own people.\nExpenence shows that man values his possessions even\nmore than his life because in the former he sees the\nmeans for perpetuabon and survival of his descendants\neven after his body 1s reduced to ashes. A new order\ncannot be established without radically changing the\nmind and atutude of men towards property and, at\nsome stage or the other, the ‘haves’ have to yield place\nto the ‘have-nots’. Earber, the comprehension passages\nwere of a moderate size in case of CDS, NDA and\nlengthy in case of CAT and some other examinatons\nconducted by management institutes. Now a one line\nPassage or very short passages compnsing only two or\nthree sentences are set. Students have need not become\nunduly answers; they must keep their cool. In fact, the\nwork has become easier than before because it is more\nconvenient to recollect ideas contained in two or three\nlines than those from along passage. Candidates should\nread the passage and compute the points in their minds.\nOn the basis of the points they can locate the main idea\nor the motive around which these points have been\nillustrated. Normally, in a short passage there is a basic\nidea and all points are given to illustrate or analyse, or\nprove the central idea. The answer must confirm to the\ncentral idea. The accessory or secondary points may\nbe true but they have a partal idenuty only and thus\nthey cannot be the correct answers. We may illustrate\nit through the MLE (Motive, Location, Elimination),\ntechnique. Let us understand the technique through an\n\nexample (Example!) from the Bank PO Examination,\nMadras, March 1996,\n\nMLE (Motive Location Elimination\n(Techniques\n\nOne of the important humanitanan by-products of\ntechnology ts the greater dignity and value that it\nimparts to human labour. In a highly industnalised\nsociety, there 1s no essental difference between\nBrahmin and Dalit, Muslim and Hindu; they are equally\nuseful and hence equally valuable, for in the industnal\nsociety individual productivity fixes the size of the pay\ncheque and this fixes the social status.\n\nThis paragraph best supports the statement that—\n\n1, There are numerous side-effects of\ntechnology\n\n2. Labour force 1s treated well in modem day\nindustnes\n\n3. Division of labour based on caste was justified\n4, There are individual differences in industnal\nproducavity\n\n34 | Objective\n\n5. Size of the pay and social status contradict\neach other\nPoints that illustrate the motive are.\n1. Industry makes no difference between caste\nand religion\n2. In industnal society social status comes from\nindividual performance\nIn fact both points reinforce the mouve of the\npassage. Refer to Fig 21\n\nEXAMPLE - 2\n\nThe only true education comes through the stmulaton\n\nof the child’s powers by the demands of the socal\nsituations in which he finds himself Through these\ndemands he 1s stmulated to act as a member of a unit,\nto emerge from his onginal narrowness of action and\nfeeling, and to conceive himself from the standpoint\nof the welfare of the group to which he belongs\nThe paragraph best supports the statement that\nreal education\n1, Is not provided in our schools today\n2. Other\npoints are secondary in identifying and reinforcing this\nbasic idea or monve.\n\nNow let us eliminate incorrect responses through\nTechnique\n\nAlternauve 1: It is wrong as this 1s not the mouve\nof the author\n\nAlternauve 2: Itis wrong,\n\nAlternanve 3: Itas the night answer as it is the\nmain idea or motive\n\nAlternative 4: It is wrong because it is a partial\nfact\n\nAlternative $: It is wrong as it is also a parnal\nfact\n\nPractice Set —12\n\n‘Throughout the ages the businessman has helped build\ncivilisabon’s great cites, provide people with luxunes\nand artists with patronage, and lift his fellow-citzens\nto understand the standard of living In the last few\ncentunes the businessman has seeded the Industral\nRevolution around the world\n\nQ The paragraph best supports the statement that\nthe businessman—\n(a)\n\n)\n\nIs accountable to the society\nIs the beneficiary of the Industral\nRevolution\n\nAnswer Alternatives\n\n1. You\ncan glean the points separately and mark them on a\nrough page or keep them in memory. On the basis of\nInformation Fliminaton and Inference formula the\ncorrect answers can be located. Thus itis wrong.\n\nThis 1s correct because the wnter explains\nwhy life does not exist in deeper layers\n(Simple Inference).\n\nThis 1s wrong as no reason has been given.\nThis is wrong as no reason has been given\nNot relevant and thus it is wrong\n\nT may hke to add that locating the motive of the\nwnter as we have done in vanation 2 will further\nhelp even in this case. After\nthis initial exercise they should learn or revise the\nfollowing rules that are given below in the form of\npracncal tips with illustranve examples. The practice\ntest must be done carefully and repeatedly to ensure\nfull competence and confidence.\n\nRules and Examples\n\n1. Certain important rules are listed below.\n\n. A past tense in the principal clause is\n\nfollowed by a past tense in the subordinate\nclause.\n\nHe saw that the clock has stopped\n\n(Incorrect)\n\nHe saw that the clock has stopped. (Correct)\n‘There are, however, two exceptions to this rule:\n(1) A past tense in the principal clause may\nbe followed by a present tense in the\nsubordinate clause, when it expresses a\nuniversal truth.\nThe teacher observed that the Earth moved\nround the Sun (Incorrect)\nThe teacher observed that the Earth moves\nround the Sun\nWhen the\nintroduced by ‘than’, even if there is\npast tense in the principal clause, it may\nbe followed by any tense required by the\nsense in the subordinate clause.\n\n(Correct)\n\nsubordinate clause is\n\n(2)\n\nHe helped him more than he helped his\nown children (Incorrect)\nHe helped him more than he helps his own\n\nchildren.\nIf two actions in a sentence are shown\n\n(Correct)\n\nhappening in the past, one after the other,\nthe tense of the action happening first\nshould be past perfect and that of the\nsecond should be past indefinite.\n\nThe patent died before the doctor arrived.\n\n(Incorrect)\nThe patient had died before the doctor\narrived. (Correct)\n\n‘Two actions in the past, one depending\non the other, should have the sequence as\nfollows:\nPast perfect + Future perfect\nHad + past participle + would + have +\npast participle\n\nIf you bad worked hard, you would have\n\nsucceeded in the examination.\n\nOr\nHad you worked hard, you sould have succeeded\n\nin the examination\n\nIf you would have practised regularly, you\nwould win the match.\nIf you had practised regularly, you would have\nwon the match.\n\n(Incorrect)\n\n(Correct)\n49. If, in a sentence, two actions are indicated\nand both are to take place in future, the\nsequence of tenses will be as follows:\n‘The principal clause in present indefinite;\nand the subordinate clause in future\nindefinite.\nIf 1 go to Delhi, I shall attend the seminar.\nNote: ‘If I go to Delhi’ is the principal clause and\n‘T shall attead the seminar’ is the subordinate clause.\nIf it will rain, I shall not attend the\nmeeting, (Incorret)\nIf it rains, I shall not attend the meeting\n(Correct)\n50. Some important points to remember regarding\nthe omission of a/an/the\n1. Before a common noun used in its\nwidest sense, e.g.\nMan ts mortal.\n2. Before names of materials.\nBronze ts a useful metal\n3. Usually before proper names.\nPatna ts the capital of Bihar.\n4. Before abstract nouns used in general sense.\nBeauty fascinates people\n5. Uses of ‘the’\n1. When the object is unique\n\nThe earth, the sky, the equator\n\n56.\n\n57.\n\n2. Before superlatives\n\nThe best, the finest, the most\n3. With proper nouns like ‘seas’, ‘rivers’,\n‘group of islands’, ‘chains of mountains’,\n‘deserts’, ‘newspapers’, ‘buildings’,\n‘religious books’, ‘gulfs’.\nThe Arabian sea, The Ganges, The Taj\n\nMahal,\n\nThe Times of India, The Ramayana\n\n4. Before a proper noun, when itis qualified\nby an adjective.\n‘The immortal Kalidas\n5. Before an adjective, when the noun is\n\nhidden.\nThe nch should be kind and helping.\n\n6. With a class of things.\n\nThe crow 1s a clever bird.\n\n8. ‘The’ should be used before both\ncomparative degrees when they are used\nin a sentence for proportion.\n\nHigher we go, cooler we feel (Incorrect)\nThe Higher we go, the cooler we feel\n(Correct)\nWhat to speak of? is incorrect; the correct\nexpression is ‘not to speak of.\nWhat to speak of running, he cannot even\nwalk. (e) No Error\n\nfor Competitive Examinations\n\n8.\n\n(b) ‘Arnve’ in place of ‘arnved’, After ‘no sooner\ndid’ the verb will be in the present tense. ‘Did’\n1s followed by the present form of verb: Did he\n‘go’? (a) ‘Ihave been working’ in place of ‘I have\nworked’. ‘Six years’ indicate that the work began\nin the past and has been conunuing Thus the\nperfect continuous tense is required\n\n10. (a) ‘The’ in place ‘a’. Here as itis the case of the\nfirst European, it is a definite case and thus the\ndefinite aruicle ‘the’ should be used. See Rule $4\n\nSet 20\n\n1. (b) ‘Is’ in place of ‘are’. ‘All we could find’ ts\nsingular and thus the verb will be singular\n\n2. (b) ‘None of the rooms’ here indicates\na singular sense and thus the verb will\nbe singular. Usually ‘none’ 1s used in singular\n\n3. (c) ‘To’ in place of ‘than’. ‘Prefer’ takes ‘to’\nafter it. See Rule 25.\n\n4. (b) ‘Who, in place of ‘whom’\n\n5. (c) ‘Might’ in place of ‘may’. The reporting\nverb is appealed. It is in the past tense and\nthus all other clauses will be in the past tense\nSee Rule 46.\n\n6. (a) ‘It being a short vacanon’ in place of being a\nshort vacation’ There should be a subject here.\n\n7. (©) A piece of useful advice\n\n8. (c) ‘Against’ in place of ‘from’. See the Rules\nof preposition.\n\n9. (c) ‘I would have’ in place of ‘I will have’. See\nRule 48\n\n10. (a) ‘Never have I’ in place of ‘Never I have’\n\nAdditional Practice Sets\n\nDirection for Questions |- 40\n\nRead each sentence to find out whether there 1s any\ngrammatical error in it. Candidates are required to make a\nchoice from muluple alternanves for each word\nblanked out, not in a sentence but in a passage. Thus\nthis test evaluates the candidate’s vocabulary power and\nhis ability to judge the overall meaning of a passage\neven when some words are missing.\n\nPractical Tip\n\nA passage consists of sentences which are logically\nrelated to one another. Therefore, first of all, read the\nwhole passage, get a rough idea of its contents and\nkeep them in your mind as a background. Concentrate\non this background to find clues. Use the following\nups as well\n1. If you know the answer, immediately nck it. No\ntechnique 1s to be adopted. The whole process\nwall work in a flash.\n2. When you are not sure of the answer, make the\nnght chore through the process of chminanon.\n\nFig. 12.1 Cloze Test Elimination Technique\n\nA.98 | Objective\n\nigi\n\nElimination Technique\n\nStep\n\n1. Judge if any of them does not fit into the\nstructure (eliminate)\n\n2) Judge if any of them does not fitin with the\nmeaning (eliminate)\n\n3. Among/between the remaining apply your\n\nawareness of the common usage or nuances\n\nof words.\n\nTry to find out a clue in the sentence or\n\nIn a sentence preceding or succeeding\n\nit Sometimes the clue 1s hidden many\n\nsentences away\n\n5. If the final choice has not been reached\neven now, apply the method of reasoning\nto hit the target\n\nFill in each of the numbered blanks in the following\n\npassage with the most suitable word out of the\nalternatives (below the passage) given against each\nnumber.\n\n1. the first flat we hved in 2. our marnage\n\nvery well. In\na sentence, one or two blanks are left out to be filled with\n‘one of the alternatives given below it. Let us examine a\nfew such sentences.\n\nOne Sentence One Blank\n\nAlthough he was a hardened cnminal, his one\nfeature was his love.\n\n(b) redeeming\n\n(d) acqunng\n\n(a) saving\n(©) recovenng\nThe answer ts (b)\n\nOne Sentence Two Blanks\n\nDisarmament and development in our ume\n\nare interrelated but\n\ndevelopment will depend on a change in the world’s\npokneal thinking,\n(a) essentaally, true\n(b) naturally, final\n(©) closely, real\n(d) mevitably, substantial\nThe answer is (c).\n\nTwo Sentences Two Blanks\n\n1. The entre class was invited to\nmodel of the proposed new city.\n\n2. There is alovely\n\nthe\n\nof the town from\n\nthe window,\n\n(a) look (b) impression\n(©) sight (d) view\n\n‘The answer is (d).\n\nNow, let us analyse the process of finding the\ncorrect answer. If you know the answer, you can make\na flash response and uck the choice. No technique is\nrequired at all. If you do not know the answer, you may\nuse the key words and elimination method.\n\n118 | Objective\nqr (Key elimination) Method\n\nK stands for key; F stands for chminaton. Every\n\nsentence must have an idea. A sentence is not merely a\ngroup of words but a meaningful group of words. So\n\nthere is an idea, a message behind every sentence\nExample Ram is an honest man\n\nHere the idea or message 1s the honesty of Ram. The\nmessage may be expressed or is generally expressed\nthrough a word or words. This we may call key word/\nwords In this sentence the key word 1s honest.\nExample The Director pointed out in favour\nof the Manager that the profitability of the plant\n\nhad since he took over\n) ansen (b) dechned\n(c) developed (d) increased\n\nHere the message or idea is profitabihty of the\nplant. The key words reflecting the idea are profitability\nand favour. Keeping profitability and favour in mind, we\ncan very easily climinate ansen, dechned and developed\nOnly increased remains unehiminated. This is the answer.\n\nAt tum\nkey words\n\nPastis to future as yesterday 18 to\n\nyou can find the answer by locating the\n\ntoday\ntomorrow\n\nday after tomorrow\n\nday before yesterday\nThe answer 1s (b), ‘tomorrow’. The key word 1s\n‘yesterday’ for which an opposite has to be found out\nSometimes you can find the answer by the process\nof clminaton\n\\ of ships was kept ready to scour\n\nthe sea in case of an emergency\n\n(a) group pack\n\n(©) battalion (d) fleet\nNow we can find out the answer by chminatng\ndifferent altematives\n(a) Group can be eliminated because ‘group’\nis used for human beings or, at least, for\nliving beings\n(b) Pack 1s used normally for playing matenals.\nA pack of cards 1s a popular expression\n\n(c) Battabon 1s normally used for a group of\n\nsolchers.\n\nThus the alternatives are chminated\n\n(b) and\n\n. leet, which is the nght\n\n‘The only alternative left is (d\nanswer\n\n‘Thus we see that sometimes we find the answer\nby locating the key words and sometimes through the\nprocess of elimination. But in most cases, we can find\nout the answer by combining both. However, we may\nuse some clues for locating key words as well as for\nchminating different alternatives\n\n‘The ‘KE Method’ can be illustrated in the figure\nbelow.\n\nStep!\n\n'\nStep IV\n\nFig. 10.1 KE Method\n\n[cves for Key Words\n\nKey words are words which assist you to get at the\nidea being expressed in the sentence. In the first\nexample, the key words are ‘hardened criminal’. Thus\nthe missing word tn this context must be a word that\n\ngives the opposite idea. Thus ‘recover:\n\nig” and ‘acquartng’\nare eliminated because they do not give the opposite\nidea of the key words ‘Saving’ and ‘redeeming’ have\nan opposite implication, but ‘saving’ has a general\nconnotation and thus 1t may be rejected. ‘Redeeming’\nimplies a contrast against a bad trait. Thus ‘redeeming’\n\nts the correct choice\n\nIn the second example, ‘disarmament’ and\n‘development’ are key words These two words imply\nman-made actions The words ‘naturally’ and ‘inevitable’\ndo not agree with the key words as natural or inevitable\ninterrelaton cannot exist between man-made actions.\n‘Essenual’1s also ehminated as the essental relavonship\nbetween two things cannot be influenced by time. It is\neternal. However, there can exist a close relationship\nbetween one thing and another in a particular situation\nor time. Thus (c) 1s the nght choice.\n\nItdepends on your common sense and intelligence\nto improvise and locate clues for a particular sentence,\nhowever, the following clues may prove helpful\n\nClues\n\n1. It may be a preposition.\n\nEvery sensible citizen abides the\nlaw of the country.\n\n(a) to (b) by\n\n(© for (@) with\n\nThe answer 1s (b), ‘by’.\n\n2 It may be a rule of grammar.\n\nI never miss a football match. I\nfond of it since my childhood\n\n(a) am (b) have been\n(c)_ has been (d) will be\nThe answer 1s (b), ‘have been’.\n\n3. It may be a substitute for a group of words\nHis wife died five years ago; since then he has\nbeen living as a\n(a) divorcee >) widower\n(©) misogynist. — (d)._ celibate\nThe answer is (b), ‘widower’, because the word\nfor ‘a man whose wife 1s dead’ 1s widower’,\n\n4, It may be an idiom or phanse\n\nThe judge decided to resign when he was\nfor promouon to Chief Jusuce.\n\n(a) passed by (b)_ passed over\n\n(©) passed off (d)._ passed out\n\nThe answer is (b) because the idiom with the\nmeaning that is required here is ‘passed over’.\n\n19\n\nTheory\n5. It may be an opposite word.\n\nMostof the issues discussed here in the meeting\nwere tavial and only a few were\n\n(a) mteresung (b) pracucal\n\n(©) significant —(d) irrelevant\n\nThe answer is (c), ‘significant’; itis the opposite\nword.\n\n6. In a simple sentence completion, you\nfill the blank with one of the words given as choices to\nyou. In this format, part of a sentence 1s given which\nneeds to be completed with one of the vanous sentence\nparts provided to you as altematves. The apparent\ndifference 1s that now we have a group of words in\nplace of a single word. This requires an understanding\nof the spint of the stem part of the sentence and the\n\nfiller part 1s to be matched with it both in terms of\nstructure and meaning,\n\nWe can find a structural clue or a semantic clue\nor both in the stem part and match the stem with the\nalternatives one after another. Only one choice will\nmatch, the rest will be wrong,\n\nWe may present it as Stem Filler Matching (SFM)\nTechnique.\n\n)\n\nFig. 11.1 Stem Filler Matching Technique\n\nA.140 | Objective English for Compe\nI SFM Technique |\n\nExample |\n\nThe more we looked at the piece of modern art,\n\n(a) 1t looked better (b) the more we like it\n\n(c) we hked it less. (d)the less we liked it\n\n(e) better we hiked it\n\nIn the stem part we have structural clue ‘the more”\n\nand iis a past tense sentence. The structural demand is\nthat the filler part also must have an expression like ‘the\nmore’ (the + a word). Taking this clue, we can chmunate\nalternatives (a), (c) and (¢). Now alternative (b) has ‘the\nmore’ and the altemative (d) has the ‘the less’. These\ntwo are correct, but (b) is further eliminated because\nthe filler is in the present tense and the stem 1s in the\npast tense. (d) is the correct answer because it is in the\npast tense. We can illustrate it by a chart\n\nExample 2\nHe 1s so lazy that he\n\n(a) always extends help to others to complete their\nwork.\n\n(b) dishkes to postpone the work that he undertakes\nto do.\n\n(©) can seldom complete his work on time\n\n¢ Examinations\n\n(d) can't delay the schedule of completing the work.\n\n(e) can’t depend on others for getting his work\ndone.\n\n(Bank PO, 1995.)\n\nIn the stem part we have a semantic clue ‘lazy’.\n\nOn the basis of this we can match with the alternatives.\n\nAlternanve (a) 1s opposite in meaning, altematve (b) 1s\n\nopposite in meaning; alternative (c) is opposite in meaning,\n\naltemanve (c) 1s irrelevant, only (c) matches in terms of\nmeaning and therefore itts the correct answer.\n\nExample 3\nThe doctor wams him that unless he gives up smoking\n\n(a) will he be able to recover\n\n(b) he will not suffer\n\n(c) his health will soon be recovered\n\n(d) he will not recover\nIn the stem part we have two clues\n\n1. unless—structural\n\n2. amoking—semantic\n\n‘Unless’ here demands a ‘not’ in the filler part\nThus alternatives (a) and (c) are wrong. Alternatives\n(b) and (d) are structurally nght, but according to\n‘smoking’ the expression ‘not suffer’ is wrong, but\n‘not recover’ is nght. Thus only (@) matches. Here\nwe have matched on the basis of two clues, one\nstructural, one semantic\n\nClues:\nx (a) It looked better\nx (b) The more we like it\nSemantic Structural x (c) We liked it less\n¥ (d) The less we liked it\nx (@) Better we liked t\n1 The more 2. Past tense\n\nFig. 11.2 SFM Technique Illustrated: Example |\n\nPractice Set —3\n\n1. In this test, you\nhave to rearrange parts of a sentence into a meaningful\nsentence or sentences into ameaningful paragraph. This\nrequires a sound knowledge of grammar, a command\nover the structure of sentences, an over-all sense of\ncomposition, and an ability to form a logical sequence\nof ideas behind sentences. This art can be gradually\nmastered by reading good books and newspapers, and\nconstant practice You must read books very carefully\ngiving full attention to the structures of the sentences.\nGradually you will begin to feel that there are internal\nrules that bind a sentence. This will help you discern\nclues for arranging parts of sentence correctly.\n\nThis question appears in a vanety of different forms\nin vanous competitive examinations. Some common\ntypes are discussed in this chapter.\n\n|. One Sentence in Parts\n\nLakhs of students\nP. on an all Incha basis\n\nQ from all over India\nR. which are held\nS. appear in the examination.\nby different recruiting orgamsations,\n\n(a) PQSR (b) SRQP\n(©) QSRP (4) QSPR\n(Combined Defence Sermces Fxcamj\n\nThis type of a question may be solved by Subject-\nVerb-Object-Qualfiers Locanon. $ +V +O + Q#L)\nLet us look at the above example:\n\n1. Lakhs of students — — subject\n2. from all over India — — qualifier of the\nsubject (Q)\n3. appear in the — verb (S)\nexamination\n4. which are held — — qualifier of\nthe verb (R)\n5. on an all India — — qualifier of\nbasis wach are held\n”)\n6. by different\nthe last part\norganisations\n\nThus the sequence ts QSRP:S+Q+V#Q+* Q#Q\n\n150 | Objective\n\nLet us analyse the following sentence in the graph\nbelow:\n\nRam who is the son of Shyam reads hurnedly the\nbook which has been watten by Premchand\n\nThe correct sentence, therefore, is: ‘Lakhs of\nstudents from all over India appear in the examinations\nwhich are held on an all-India basis by different\nrecruing organisations’,\n\niglish for Compe!\n\nLook at the structure of a sentence. The basic\nform is like this\n\nHere, the subject, the verb and the object, all three\nhave qualifiers\n\n¢ Examinations\n\nNow itis clear from the chart that, in a sentence,\nthere can appear a subject, a verb, an object and\nqualifiers as well. You have to locate and identify each\nquahfier and attach 1t to its subject\n\nThe steps may be summansed as follows\n\n1. subject of the sentence;\n\n2. qualifiers of the subject, if any;\n\n3. verb;\n\n4. qualifiers of the verb, if any;\n\n5. object, and\n\n6. qualifiers of the object, if any.\n\nThis may be descnbed as the Sul\n\nObject + Qualifiers Location Technique\n\nSentence\n1\nSubject Verb Object\n1 J 1\nRam reads a book\nSentence\n1\nr T T 1\nSubject Qualifier Verb Object\n1 4 4 J\nRam, who isthe son of Shyam teads a book\n\nHere, who is the son of Shyam, is the qualifier of Ram, the subject.\n\nSentence\nr T T 1\nSubject Verb Qualifier Object\n\n1 4 4\nRam reads hurriedly\n\nHere, hurmedty is the qualifier of reads, the verb.\n\nSubject Qualifier Verb\n4 1 4\nRam who is the reads\nson of\nShyam\n\n2. Sentences with the First and the\nLast Part Given\n\nIn the passage below the first and the last sentences\n\nQualifier Object Qualifier\n4 4 .\nhurriedly the book which has\nbeen written\nby Premchand\n\nare numbered 1 and 6. The rest four sentences are\nnumbered as PQRS and they are not in correct\nsequence. Rearrange them\n\n1. India’s message has always been of love and\npeace\n\nP. Our Budha was the hght of Asia\n\nQ. Ithas been a source of light and wisdom to the\nrest of the world\n\nR. Ashoka moved by the horrors of Kalinga war\ngave the message of non-violence.\n\na\n\n‘The greatest apostle of non-violence in recent\nyears was Gandhi\n6 He shook the foundation of the Brush rule in\nIndia through non-violence\n(a) PSQR b) RPQS\n(© SRQP ) QPRS\nSuch questions can be solved by applying,\n\nIdea to Sentence Stepping (ITSS) Method\n\nA paragraph consists of sentences which are connected\nto one another logically and semanucally. Thus, first of\nall, read every sentence carefully, discern the meaning\nof the passage, then form a sequence at the level\nof idea (the meaning). Then arrange the sentences\naccordingly. You can do this by improvising on some\nclues. Clues can be found out on the basis of your\ngeneral knowledge, your sense of language and your\nabihty to correlate ideas and structures.\n\nIn the above example, we can find many clues.\nThe first sentence 1s given. It is about India. Now look\nat the alternatives The second sentence must follow\nthe first at the level of meaning and also at the level\nof structure\n\n(a) P. begins with our\n(b) Q begins with #\n(c) R. begins with Ashoka\n(d) S. begins with the\n\nNow #f1s the only pronoun that can be used for\nIndia and thus the second sentence should be Q In\nother words, our answer should begin with Q. The\nalternative (b) begins with Q and thus this is the choice.\n\nWe can find another clue by looking at the last\nsentence. It tells us about someone who shook the\nfoundanon of the Bnush rule in India through non-\nviolence. All of us know that Mahatma Gandhi was the\nman who did it. Thus S 1s the sentence that must come\nbefore the last sentence. In other words, our correct\nchoice must end with S. The alternative (b) ends with S.\n\n51\n\nWe can find another clue to decide which 1s the\n\nTheory\n\ncorrect answer between (a) and (c). The sense of time\ngives us aclue. There are references to Buddha, Ashoka\nand Gandhi. Keeping their penods in mind. We can\narrange sentences by putting the sentence with Ashoka\njust before the sentence that mentons Gandhi. ‘Thus\nS must be preceded by R. Thus the correct alternative\nis D because $ is preceded by Rin this arrangement\n\n3. Several Sentences Without Any\nDefinite Beginning or End\nRearrange the following six sentences, A,B,C, D, E and\n\nF, in the proper sequence so as to form a meaningful\nparagraph and then answer the questions given below.\n\nA. Miss Sullivan arnved at the Keller home when\nHelen was seven.\n\nB. The deaf and\n\ncommunicate verbally.\n\nblind Helen learned to\n\nC. But, eventually, Miss Sullivan's effort was\nrewarded.\n\nD. Before Helen Keller was two years old, she lost\nher sight and her heanng\n\nE. Miss Sullwan worked closely with Helen, her\nnew student.\n\nF. At tumes, the teacher became frustrated\n(P.O. Examimation, BSRB, Delhi, Lucknow,\n\nBaroda, 1992)\n\n1. Which sentence should come last?\n\nA B. Cc\n\nD E. F\n2. Which sentence should come first?\n\nA B Cc\n\nD E. F\n3. Which sentence should come second?\n\nA B Cc\n\nD E. F.\n4. Which sentence should come third?\n\nA B. Cc\n\nD E. F.\n5. Which sentence should come fourth?\n\nA B. Cc\n\nD E. F\n\nA.152 | Objective English for Compe\n\n6. Which sentence should come fifth?\n\nA B Cc\nD. E F.\nMethod The same method of arranging the\nparagraph first at the level of idea and then at the\nlevel of sentences should be followed Since the first\nand the last sentences are not given, the first step 1s\nto locate the beginning sentence\nFirst, find some clues. For instance, in this\nparagraph, we may find a clue in the reference to ime\nThe paragraph 1 about Helen and Miss Sulhvan. The\nfirst thing that happened in relation to time was the\nfact that Helen lost her sight before she was two years\nold. Miss Sullivan arnved when Helen was seven. The\nother incidents took place later Thus, it ts logical that\nthe first sentence should be D—the sentence telling us\nthat Helen lost her sight before she was two years old.\nAfter that we can arrange other sentences according to\nthe ume sequence. The sequence s DAE FCB. All\nquestions may be answered now:\n\n1B 2D 3A\n4£E S.F 6.C\n\nTime-Saver Clue Qualifier Elimination (CQE)\nTechnique\n\nIn addition to the above devices we may also try a\ntume-saver technique. This technique will be directly\napplicable to type one and type two and will also be\nhelpful in type three of sentence arrangement. In\ntype three question, five or six sentences are given in\na disorderly sequence. In most cases the first and the\nlast sentences are specified and the remaining four are\nto be arranged. Four answer sets are provided as the\nalternauve choice to the candidates. Only one choice 1s\ncorrect which the candidates have to spot out.\n\nFirst of all, we shall find some clues in the set.\nAbout clues we shall discuss later. On the basis of the\nclue we shall locate a guahfier. We shall define guakfier as\na sentence that tells us something about the following\nsentence. That following sentence is the gwalified\nsentence. The qualifier will be placed by the side of\nthe qualified. It 1s just possible that there may be two\nqualifiers of the same sentence. In that case we have to\ndecide the preference on the basis of sequence\n\n¢ Examinations\n\nNow there are three possibilities\n\nPossibility (1) Our qualifier may be qualifying the first\nsentence. In this case our answer choice begin with\nthe Quailfier\n\n1. It as regrettable that there is widespread\ncorruption in the country at all levels.\n\nP. Clues can be located or improvised with the help\nof grammatical rules. However, pronouns, like be, she,\nat, this, that, these, those, arucle a and the, definers lke\nactually, finally, another connectors like but, yet, both, also,\nand nether nor, or, however, nevertheless, and adverbs like\nformally, realy, can be of definite help in identifying\nthe qualifiers on the basis of which we may select our\ncorrect answers as illustrated above\n\nExample (A)\n\n1. (a)\n\n(a9)\nhs |\n[aa\nLW\nKE\noa\n<=\na a\nO\n\nSentence improvement 1s a vanation upon spotting\nerror In a spotting error question you are asked to\nlocate the error and tick the answer. Here, you are\nasked to evaluate the highhghted part of the sentence\nand improve it by making it correct if its incorrect, or\nappropnate, if it 1s inappropnate or a better sentence\nby changing it with a choice if it can be done so. This\nis therefore a more complex exercise. The error or\ninappropnateness may be in any part of speech, or in\ntense verb form, or sentence pattern, or in the use of\nan idiom or a phrase or proverb.\n\nIn fact, the mmprovement can be done by correcting,\nthe error or making the meaning clear We can illustrate\nit with this figure:\n\nLook at the itaheised part of cach sentence. Below\ncach sentence are given possible substitutions for the\nttaheised part. If one of them — a, bor c makes\nthe sentence correct, or 1s better than the italicised\npart or makes the meaning clear indicate your response\nagainst the corresponding a, bor ¢ If none of the\nsubstitutions improves the sentence, indicate d as your\nanswer.\n\n() ——orx\nImprovement\n\nby Clanty\n\n1. They are waiting for her since morning\n(a) have been waiting\n(b) were waiting\n(c) wait\n(d) No umprovement\nHere in this sentence, the error is in the\ntense form. The correct answer is (a), “have\n\nA.176 | Objective\n\nbeen waiting” because the sentence shows a\ncontinuous sense requinng the present perfect\ncontnuous tense form.\n\niglish for Compe!\n\n2. If you would have seen the movie, you would\nhave enjoyed it (Bank PO exam)\n(a) had you been seen\n(b) af you have had seen\n(©) had you seen\n(d)_ No correction required\n\n‘The answer is (c), ‘had you seen’. The error\nhere hes in the tense sequence. According to the\nrules of grammar, when there are two actions\nof past probability, one dependent on the other,\nthe sequence should be as follows; past perfect\n+ future perfect.\n\nHad + past parnesple + would\n\n1 2 3 4 $s\n\npast participle\n\nThus the correct form of the sentence in\nthis example will be:\n\nHad you seen the movie, you would have\nenjoyed it\n\nHere, in both cases, we find that the answer 1s\ngiven on the basis of the errors of grammar.\n\nIn majonty of the questions indifferent\nexaminations can be answered by\n\ncorrecting the errors, but im certain cases the\nanswer can be given on the basis of\n\nfinding correct meaning of the highlighted part\n\n{ EXAMPLE\n\nMy neighbour was caught stealing, he now has to face\nthe music. (a) 14. &)\n15. @) 15. ) 15) 15) 15 (4)\n\n+\nho |\n[aa\nLW\nE\nQa\n<=\na\nO\n\n[iocobulory\n\nRelevance of Vocabulary\n\nWords constitute the ean trtalof language. A speech or\na wntten passage, however short or large, 1 essentially\na group of sentences and a sentence, in its turn, 1s a\ngroup of words. A nch stock of words thus becomes\nquite logically an essential prerequisite of language\nability development. All competitive examinations take\na direct test of vocabulary under items like Synonym,\nAntonym, Sentence Complenon and Cloze Test.\nBesides, Comprehension and Sentence Arrangement\nrequire indirectly a competent understanding of words,\ntheir meaning, nuances and usage\n\nHow to Build a Powerful Vocabulary\n\nLearnings difficult, forgetting easy. [tis a real challenge:\nhow to leam words and not forget them? We can devise\nseveral methods in accordance with our habits, ving\nconditions and ume availability. However, the basic\nfactor that remains germane in every case 1s repennon\nor recalling of words at short intervals, so that they\nare etched deep in our memory. It is advisable to learn\n\nwords from newspapers, speeches, magazines and\ngood books because through sentences you can have\nan idea of their use and vanations rather than simple\ndenotations. Words change their nuances according\nto the context. For example, we can phrase out two\nsentences from the word expose.\n\n1. Several leaders have been exposed for their\n\ninvolvement in scams.\n2. If you go to a good institute you will expose\nyourself to a better academic atmosphere.\n\nWhile in the first sentence it has a negative nuance,\nin the second it has a positive connotation. Thus both\nmemonisation of words and understanding of their\nnuances and usage are necessary to develop a nich\ntreasure of vocabulary.\n\nWe may adopt the following methods.\n1. Card System Make cards of a manageable size.\nLeam 15 new words everyday from newspapers and\nmagazines and wnte them on the card along with their\nmeanings. Gradually, cards will pile up. Keep them\non your reading table. Spare five minutes everyday\nanytime dunng the day. Shuffle them, give a look at\nthe top card and revise the words. Next tme another\ncard will be on the top. Thus you will go on adding\n\nA.200 | Objective\n\nand revising More and more words will become your\npossession\n\n2. Calendar System Just select a good old calendar\nwith as many pages as possible. Hang it on the wall of\nyour reading room from the reverse side. Wate fifty\ngood selected words on each page. Look at the page\neveryday anytime dung the day whenever you feel\nfree. Change the page after a week. Looking at the same\npage for a week means deepening your acquaintance\nwith the same set of words several times. ‘This way, you\n\niglish for Compe!\n\nwill be revising a lot of words without strain. Words\nwill etch themselves deep into your memory.\n\n3. Word Profile Making a word profile may work\nas a powerful and enduring technique to master words\nMake a separate profile of every important word on\na separate card. Write its meaning, its synonyms, its\nantonyms, tts other parts of speech, its usage and its\ndifferences from other synonyms. We may use some\npnvate symbols for our understanding\n\nP: Poste A word which should always be preferably\nused in a good sense 1s a P word.\n\nExample Alacrity, aplomb, benign.\n\nN: Negattte A word which should always be preferably\nused in a negative sense is a N word\n\nExample Cahoots (in collaboration). We cannot\nsay ‘in cahoots with and honest person’, but we\ncan say ‘in cahoots with a dishonest person’\n\nNT: Neutral\nsenses according to the need of the context\n\nExample Catalyst. Tyranny acts as a catalyst for\nterrorism. A challenge acts as a catalyst for a brave\nman to show his worth.\n\nC: Concrete\n\nA word which can be used in both\n\nThere are certain words which have a\ndefinite shape and size, a physical entity. Such a word\nisa C word\nExample\nA: Abstract\nabstract form. They exist only on the level of idea\nSuch a word 1s an A word.\n\nExample Kindness, hiatus, aplomb\n\nO: Oper\nin both concrete and abstract senses. Such a word 1s\nan O word.\n\nLamp. A lamp is a concrete thing.\n\nThere are certam words which have an\n\nThere are certain words which can be used\n\n¢ Examinations\n\nExample\n‘The prisoner is in fetters. Fetter also means some\nkind of bondage. India is in fetters of poverty and\n\nFetter. Fetter means a chain of iron\n\nunemployment\n\nFan. A fan in the concrete sense means a device\nto give air. In the abstract sense it means to enhance\nFor example, It is a new fan, Suppression fans protest\nS: Speafic\nspecific meaning, Such a word 1s a $ word\nExample\npart of a body before doing a ntual\n\nThere are certain words which have a\nAblunion. It means cleaning of body or a\n\nFranchise. It means the nght to vote.\nL: Lang There are certain words which can be used\nonly for ving things\nExample Greganous. Man is greganous. Such a\nword 1s a L, word.\nNL: Not dreng\nused only for non-hving things\nExample\n\nargument. We cannot say that Ramesh is a cogent man.\n\nThere are certain words which can be\nCogent. We can say that it is a cogent\nU: Universal There are certain words which can be\nused for both living and non-hving\n\nExample\nItas an excellent idea or proposal. Both sentences are\ncorrect. Such a word 1s a U word.\n\nExcellent. Mr. Sinha 1s an excellent man.\n\nThese are our personally developed symbols. We\ncan make symbols according to our choice. The idea\nis to catalogue more and more usage of words. In the\nabove manner we can make profiles of words Words\nwhich are leamt in such details will remain in memory\nfor ever or at least for a long time\n\nModel |\nFantastic\n\nPart of speech Adjective\n\nOther parts Fantasneally\n\nof Speech (Adverb)\n\nSynonyms Excellent, large, won-\nderful, strange, unlikely,\nunbehevable.\n\nAntonyms Common, ordinary, small,\n\nfamthar\n\nTheory | A.201\n\nJSyecaym\n\nA synonym is a word which has the same, or nearly the\nsame meaning which another word has. Let us look at\nsome such words.\n\n1. Choose the word nearest in meaning to the\n\ngiven word\n\n(a) strong &) grave\n\n(c) weak (d) showy\n\n‘The answer is (c), weae, as this is similar in\nmeaning to fragile\n\nEsotenc\n\n(a) far (b) popular\n\n(©) allunng (d) pavate\n\nThe answer is (d), private. This 1s not exactly\nequivalent to esofere but itis nearest in meaning,\namong the altematives.\n\nThe test can be given directly, as shown above,\nor even through a sentence.\n\n2. The itahcised word or phrase 1s followed by\nfour words marked (a), (6), (c) and (d). Choose\nthe one which is the nearest in meaning to the\ngiven word.\n\nHe used to regal us with anecdotes\n(a) flatter (b) bore\n(co) fix (d) entertain\nThe answer 1s (d), entertain. It 1s the nearest in\nmeaning among the alternatives.\nHe ts beheved to be a very industrious worker.\n(a) successful) sensible\n(©) punctual (d) diigent\nThere answer is (d), dihgent; 1t 1s similar in\nmeaning,\n\nClues or Tips\n\n1. When the test 1s made through a sentence, try\nto find the key word and then use the technique\nof chmimation.\n\nHe had the nee to suggest that I was cheating\n(a) strength (b) capacity\n\n(©) audacity (d) courage\n\nThe key word here 1s cheating and the sense\ntherefore is negative. Now strength, capacty and\ncourage—all these have a positive nuance. Thus\nthey have to be eliminated. Audaaty is therefore\nthe nght answer.\n\nSuppose we have been given this sentence:\n\nHe had the sere to face the robbers all alone.\n(a) strength (b) capacity\n\n(©) audacity (d) courage\n\nHere the key word 1s face and the sense 1s\npositive. Thus cowrage, becomes the nght choice.\nThe same word nerte has different nuances\nin different contexts. Thus one should try to\nlocate the sense behind the itahcised word.\n\n2. If the test is made directly, try to use the same\n\nform of word and tense.\nWane.\n\n(a) dechine (b) tired\n(©) dead (@) shine\n\nNow sane is used here as a verb. Tired and dead\nare not in the verb form but in the adjecuve\nform and thus they can be eclminated. The\ncorrect answer 1s dechne which 1s also a verb\nhere\n\nBaphse\n\n(a) chnsten\n\n(b)_ holy\n\n(c) dehumanise\n\n(d) something that had been ostracised.\n\n‘The word baptise is in the present tense; it is\na verb and itis in the active voice. Hof is an\nadjective. Something that had been ostracsed is in\nthe past tense. Thus, both these alternatives are\nchmunated. The answer 1s christen as debumanise 1s\nan antonym.\n\n3. One of the frequent tricks used by the examiner\nts to include an antonym in choices given as\nanswers for a synonyms question\nHence be careful whether you are looking for\n\nan antonym or a synonym\n\nBenedichon.\n(a) nse (b) blessing\n(©) curse (a) prayer\n\nThe altemative (c), curse, is an antonym here.\nOne should be alert in such a case.\n\nPractice Set —1\n\nAgainst each key word are given some suggested\nmeanings. Note that the given word and the answer must\nbelong to the same parts of speech.\nPragmatic\n(a) angry ()) quixone\n(©) colourful (d) pungent\nHere the choice (c), colourful 1s a noun while\nthe word pragmatic is an adjective and thus this\nalternauve may be rejected. Note that the given word and the answer must\nbelong to the same tense form.\nPerceived:\n(a) ignored (b) created\n(©) apprehend —(d)_ conceive\nHere the alternatives apprehend and conceive are in\nthe present tense and thus they can be rejected.\nIgnored gives an opposite meaning and it is also\nin the past tense as the word pereied 1s. Thus it\nas the correct answer.\n\n3.\n\n4.\n\nMake sure that the given word and the answer\n\nare in the same voxe. This\n\n18, therefore, the correct answer,\n\nD. You can solve this queston by\nthe following method\n\nTherefore C-D combination 1s the automatic\nanswer.\n\nFirst of all, match A with B and see if it is. a synonym\nor antonym and chminate this combination. Sumilarly,\n\niglish for Compe!\n\nrepeat the matching process for A-C combination\nand then to A-D combinanon. (1) 50 (5)\n\nSs\nhn |\n[aa\nLW\nE\nQa\n<=\na\nO\n\nLocation-€limination-Selection\nLES) Technique\n\nThis 1s also a vanation of the vocabulary test. In this\ntype of test two lists are given, Last [and Last ID Last I\ncontains four words and List II contains six meanings.\nYou have to locate the correct meanings of all these\nfour words chminating the two extra meanings or\nexpressions Thereafter, you have to select the correct\nanswer from the four choices provided: A, B,C, D. All\nthese four choices have sets of numbers. The correct\nchoice contains all four correct meanings of the four\ndifferent words.\n\nThis type of test has been introduced by UPSC for\nAssistant Provident Fund Commissioners Examination\nand it is now being followed by other recuiting Boards.\n\nApparently this 1s a difficult test. In fact, it re-\nquires a knowledge of all four words, their meaning\n\nand usage given in the list. The simple logic 18 that you\nshould develop a good vocabulary, and this could be\ndone only by extensive reading,careful listening to good\nspeeches and by giving full attention to the usage of\nwords in vanous contexts. Above all, dnlling exercises\nmust be done regularly to sharpen your recalling and\nmemonsing abilities.\n\nHowever, we can solve this problem through a\nlocation, elimination and selection technique.\n\nYou can move from the word list to the\nmeaning hist or even twr-rersia, sometimes meanings\ncan strike and give you some idea to locate the\ncorresponding word.\n\nIn a majonty of cases, the answer can be found\nat Stage 2 or 3. Thus we can locate the correct answer\nchoice without knowing the exact meaning of all four\nwords. But, as good can-didates, you should build up a\nstrong vocabulary and develop a command over usage\nof words.\n\nA.236 | Objective English for Compe\n\nStage I\n(Select the easiest word in hist I and locate its\nmeaning in hist II and get its number)\nStage 2\n(Find the located number in the answer table)\nStage 3\n(With the help of the located number eliminate\nwrong chotces and select the correct answer)\nStage 4\n(Select the next easy/known word and locate its\nanswer if there are two or three answer choices)\nStage 5\n(Select still another word and repeat the same\nprocess till you find the answer)\n\nFataton +\n\nList 1 List II\nA. We can make use of the common\nspelling rules that may help the learners.\n\npre Word endings\n\n* No Enghsh word ends in ’v’ except spiv Use\n‘ve’ instead.\n\n* No English word ends in 'f. Use ‘ge’ or ‘dge’ instead.\n\n* No Enghsh word ends in 4’. Use ‘y’ instead.\nExceptions: macaroni, spaghetn, vermicelli\n(Itahan) and tam (short for taxicab).\n\n* The word endings ‘dge’, ‘tch’, may only be used\nafter a short vowel eg badge, hedge, lodge,\nfetch, Dutch, catch. Exceptions to this rule are:\nmuch, such, nch, which\n\n+ We double 1, ‘f”, ‘s’ and ‘2’, after a single vowel\nat the end of a short word.cg call, tell, toss, miss,\nsnff, stuff, fizz, jazz, Exceptions to this spelhng,\nrule: us, bus, gas, if, of, this, yes, plus, nil, pal.\n\nTwo-syllable words that end with ‘s’.\n\nMany words that end with ‘s’ have the stress on the\nfirst syllable, e.g, cnsis, bonus, crocus, circus, litmus and\nfungus .... These follow the rule the use only one's’ at\nthe end of the word.\n\nIf the stress 1s on the final syllable of a disyllabic\nword, the final's’ 4s’ doubled, e.g, address, redress, discuss,\nunless, dismiss, undress, remiss, amiss, recall, rebuff. Also,\nthe letters're’, ‘dis’ and ‘un’ in the examples above are\nprefixes, and the rule when adding these prefixes ts to\nkeep the base word the same and just add the prefix.\n\nTwo-syllable words that end with ‘I’.\n\nWith regard to final ‘1’, and final ‘f’, the rule\nconceming prefixes applies, eg, refill, recall, rebuff.\n\nIn words where there 1s no prefix, the question of\nwhere the stress lies does not apply. ‘Fulfil’ and ‘unnl’\nhave the stress on the final syllable and many words,\neg pistol, petrol, petal, metal, lintel, lentil and pencil\nhave the stress on the first syllable.\n\nA.266 | Objective\n\nThe letters ‘ck’ are used at the end of one-syllable\n\niglish for Compe!\n\nbase words that have a single short vowel, eg, sack,\nstick, clock\n\nUse only a single letter ‘c’ at the end of words like\npicnic, cubic, metnc and anthmenic\n\nJrule 2: Making Plurals\n\n* Regular plurals are made by adding ‘s’, eg,\ndogs, horses, monkeys, cliffs\n\n* To form plurals of words with a hissing ending,\nadd ‘es’. Use suffix ‘es’ after s, x, 2, sh, ch, ss\nin words like bonuses, boxes, fizzes, wishes,\nchurches and misses\n\nTo make nouns ending in a single ‘f” plural change\nthe ‘f? to ‘V before adding ‘es’ to form the plural,\ncg, loaf, loaves, wolf, wolves, shelf, shelves\nExceptions to this rule are: dwarfs, roofs, chiefs\n\nWords ending in an ‘o’ preceded by a consonant\n\nusually end in suffix ‘es’ to form the plural,\neg, potato-es, volcano-es, torpedo-es. Some\nexceptions are: pianos, solos, Eskimos\n\nJPue 3: Adding suffixes\n\n* Short words ending in both a single vowel\nand a single consonant always double the last\nconsonant before adding a suffix (ending)\nbeginning with a vowel, eg, hop, hopped,\nhopping; flat, flatter, flatten; win, winner,\nwinning, bit, bitty, bitten. We sometimes refer\nto these base words as 1,1,1 words because they\nhave one vowel, one consonant after the vowel,\nand one syllable. Examples of vowel suffixes\n\nare: ed, es, ing, en, y, al, able and ible.\n\nDo not double the final consonant when\nthe base word has two vowels or two final\nconsonants, ¢.g, leaf, leafy, shout, shouung,\nfool, foolish, self, selfish, mend, mending.\n\nDrop the final ‘e’ from a root word before\nadding a suffix beginning with a vowel, but\nkeep it before a consonant suffix. eg, love,\nloving, lovely, taste, tasting, tasty; nde, bubble,\nbubbling, bubbly dnve, dnving, drver, rattle,\n\n¢ Examinations\n\nrattled, rattling,\n\n‘Ful’ 1s a suffix added onto the end of a root\n(or base) word. It has only one T, e.g, hopeful,\nuseful, cheerful. Because this suffix begins with\na consonant, just add it to the base (root) word,\nwithout changing the root word. Notice that we\nkeep the silent e in ‘hopeful’ and ‘useful’ above\n* Other consonant suffixes follow the same\n\nrule as above. They include ‘ment’, ‘ly’, ty,\n‘ness’, Tess’. They are used to make words hke\n‘statement’, ‘lonely’, ‘cruelty’, ‘lateness’ and\n‘homeless’. Remember to keep the final ‘e’ of\nthee base word in word spellings ike this\n\nIf a word ends in a consonant plus 'y, change the\n¥’ to ¥ before adding any suffix except ‘ing’. ¢g\nlady, laches; party, partes; ‘heavy’, hheamter’, heaviest’;\n‘marry’, ‘marned’, ‘funny’, ‘funnily’, but there are\nsome excepbons, ¢.g ‘cry’, ‘crying’, ‘hurry’, ‘hurrying’.\n\nIf a base word ends with the letters ‘oy’, ‘ay’,\nor ‘ey’, do not change the final ‘y’ to ‘i’ when\nadding any vowel suffix, e.g annoying, played,\ndisplayed, surveyed, boyish. Exceptions to this\nare the words ‘said’, ‘paid’ and ‘laid’\n\n| Jrue 4: Adding prefixes\n\n* The letters ‘al’ are a prefix followed by another\nsyllable in words like ‘also’, ‘already’, ‘always’,\n‘although’. Although the word ‘all? has two Ils,\nthe prefix ‘al’ has only one 1°\n\nOther prefixes are: re, pre, de, in, um, un,en, under,\n\nover, dis, and mus, as in rethink, preset, deactivate,\nincapable, impossible, undecided, enslave,\nunderachieve, overcook, discontinue and so on.\n\nJrue 5: Using ‘soft’ ¢ and ‘soft’ g\n\n* When ‘c’ is followed by ‘e’, ‘1, or ‘y’, it says ‘s\"\nOtherwise it says ‘’, eg, city, centre, ceiling,\ncircle, cycle, receive, access\n\n* When ‘g’ 1s followed by ‘ec’, 4, or ‘y’ it says 4\nOtherwise it says ‘g’ as in gold, e.g, gentle, gant,\ngymnastic, gyroscope. Exceptions: get, begin,\ngan, pive, gear, gees\n\nJrue 6: for using ‘w' and ‘qu’ |\n\n+ When ‘w’ or ‘qu’ comes before ‘a’ it often says\n(wo) or (kwa) as in wash, want, wander, wallet,\nquarrel and quads\n\nWhen ‘w’ comes before ‘or’ it often says (wer)\nas in worm, word, work, worth, worship\nExceptions include worry, worned, wore, worn\n\nWhen ‘w’ or ‘qu’ come before ‘ar’, it often says\n(wor) or (quor) as in war, ward, warden, quarter,\nquart.\n\nThe sound (kw) 1s wnitten as ‘qu’. It never\nstands by itself. The letter uis a “silent” partner\nItts not a “sounded vowel” when used after the\nletter q\n\nRule 7: Using ‘i’ and ‘e’ together\nto make one sound\n\n* 4’ comes before ‘e’ when it 1s pronounced ‘ee’,\nexcept when it follows ’c’, e.g, bnef, field, pest,\nreceive, deceive, ceiling\n\n+ ‘e’ comes before 4’ when sounded like (ay) as in\nneighbour and weigh\n\nThe letters 1 and ¢ are someumes used together\nand sounded as separate vowels, e.g. in diet, quiet and\nchent\n\nRule 8 : Using ‘ti’, ‘ci’ or ‘si’ to\nmake the sound (sh)\n\n+ ‘n’, ‘a’, ‘si’, are three spellings most frequently\nused to say ‘sh’ at the beginning of alll syllables\nexcept the first, e.g, nation, pabent, torrenual,\ninfecbous, spacious, ancient, opucian, financial,\n\ntension, session, admission, pension, division.\n\nRule 9 : Doubling ‘I’ and 't’ in multi-\nsyllabic words before suffixes\n\n* In words ending in a single ‘after a single\nvowel, double the ‘’ before adding a suffix\n\nregardless of accent, eg, cancelled, levelling,\ntravelled, signalling, metallic\n\nIf a word of more than one syllable ends in\na‘, preceded by a single vowel, and has the\naccent (stress) on the last syllable, double the\nfinal consonant, e.g, permit, permitted; admit,\nadmitted, regret, regretted, but do not double\nthe final tin words hike visit, visited, benefit,\nbenefited because the stress is on the first\nsyllable of the root or base word.\n\nAnother important point to keep in mind is to\nwatch some easily confusable words.\n\nEnghsh words have identical or similar pronunciations\nbut different spellings. It tells whether\nthe subject of verb acts or ts acted upon. English has\ntwo voice: active and passe.\n\nA verb is in the active voice when its subject 1s\nthe doer, of the acton For example, the verb 1s in\nactive voice in the sentence Han reads a book because\nthe subject; Hart performs the action; reads.\n\nA verb is in pasare voice when its subject receives the\naction. For example, in the sentence The book was read by\nHan. The subject, book, receives the acuon, sus read.\n\nLet us study the two voices in detail.\n\nActive Voice\nA verb is in Active Voice when its form shows that the\nsubject does something.\n\nIn sentences wntten in Active Voice, the subject\nperforms the action expressed in the verb; Le. the\nsubject acts,\n\n1. The dog dit the boy.\n2. Pooja szi/ present her research at the conference.\n\n3. Scientists ave conducted expenments to test the\nhypothesis.\nIn each example the subject of the sentence\nperforms the action expressed in the verb.\n\nConstruction of the Active Voice\nThe normal pattern 1s of a sentence in active voice 1s:\n(S + Vj or(S+V +O)\nwhere S = Subject, V = Verb and O = Object\nLet us study this pattem in the following examples:\n\n1. The dog — bit the boy\nSubject Verb Obpect\n2. Pooja will present the research\nat the conference\nSubject Verb Obpect\n3. Scenusts have conducted — experiments to\ntest the\nhypothesis.\nSubject Verb Obpect\nPassive Voice\n\nIn sentences written in Passive Voice, the subject\nreceives the action expressed in the verb; Le. the subject\n\nA.274 | Objective English for Compe\n\nis acted upon. The agent performing the acbon may\nappear in the phrase by the... or the phrase or may be\nomitted. The passive contains some form of the verb\nbe such as 4s, #as, were, or been, plus the part participle of\nthe verb. Only fransitite verbs or verbs that take a direct\nobject can be charged into passive vore\n\n1. The boy wus bitter by the dog\n\nResearch 27// be presented by Pooja at the\nconference\n\nis)\n\n3. Experiments hare been conducted to test the\nhypothesis\n\nNote: agent performing action has been omitted\n\nActive voice: Subject + Active verb + object\n\nae\nPassive voice: Subject + passive verb + ‘by’ + agent\n\nConstruction of the Passive Voice\n\nThe structure of the Passive Voice 18 as follows\n\n¢ Examinations\n\n{Subject + Auxiliary Verb (be) + Main Verb (past\nparticiple)}\nLook at the examples given in the table below.\n\nUse of the Passive Voice\nWe use the Passive Voice when\n\n1. Helping Verb Past by + Object\nParticiple\n\nOther Types of Sentences\n\nThere are few verbs which take two objects, a Direct\nObject and an Indirect Object.\n\nFor such sentences, there are two forms of Passive\nVoce sentences.\n\n1. Verb with two Objects\n(Active Voice) I gave bim five rupees\n(Passive Voice) Five rupees were given to him by\nme\nHe was given five rupees by me\n(Active Voice She has told are the trath,\n@, Voie) I was told the truth by her.\nThe truth was told to me by her.\n(Active Voice) ‘They lent me their car.\n(Passive Voice) I was lent a car by them.\nA car was lent to me by them,\n(Active Voice! The government has sent hem a\nmessage.\n> Voice) He was sent a message by the\n\ngovernment.\nA message was sent to him by the\ngovernment.\n\n2. Imperative Sentence (Commands and\n\nRequests)\n\nThere 1s a special formula for changing an imperative\n\nsentence into Passive Voice.\n\n(Acnye Voice) Please open the door.\n\n@. Voice) Let the door be opened by you\nHence the sentence in Acuve Voice has been\n\nchanged into a sentence in Passive Voice. “Let” 1s used\nto convert the Active Voice sentence into a Passive\n\nVorce sentence\n\nDo not mistake me.\n\nLet me not be mistaken.\n\nCut your nails.\n\nLat your nails be cut\n\nDo not ignore my suggestions\nLetnot my suggestions be ignored\n\n3. With Modal Verbs\n\n‘The verbs cam, could, may, might, and must are known as\nModal Verbs, as per the following\n\nOTHER EXAMPLES|\n\n(Active Voice) You can take the book\n\n(Passive Voxe) The book can be taken by you\n\n(Active Voice) Everybody must obey the rules\n\n(Passive Voice) The rules must be obeyed by eve-\nrybody.\n\n(Active Voice) They might have studied their les-\nsons\n\n(Passive Voce) Their lessons might have been\nstudied by them\n\n(Active Voice) He may not accept this plan.\n\n(Passive Vox) This lesson may not be accepted\n\nby him\n\n4. Phrasal Verbs\n\nSome verbs, with certain meaning, are always used\nwith prepositions In such situations, the prepositions\nshould not be omitted in the sentences\n\nEXAMPLES\n\n(Active Voice) We are looking at the game\n\n(Passive Vos) The game is being Jooked af by us\n\n(Active Voice) The fire-bnigade put out the fire\n\n(Passive Vos) The fire was put out by the fire-bn-\ngade\n\n(Active Voxe) The government will set ap a fac-\ntory.\n\n(Passive Vox) A factory will be set up by the gov-\nemment\n\n5, With Clauses\n\nIn some sentences, instead of a word or a phrase, a\nclause may be the object of a verb. In such cases there\nare two ways of changing the sentence into Passive\nVoice.\n\nA. By making the clause itself as the subject in\nthe Passive Voice\n\n(Active Voice) Lhope that he atll realize bis mistake.\n\nTheory 79\n(Passive Voice) That bis mistake will be realized is\nhoped by us.\n(Active Voice) Lexpected that they would take up the\nmatter.\n(Passive Voice) That the matter would be taken up by\n\nthem was expected by me.\nB. By substituting the clause with pronoun it.\n\n(Asuuye Voice) The Romans expected that they\nwould conquer Carthage.\n\n(Passive Voice) It was expected by the Romans that\nCarthage would be conquered by\nthem,\n\n(Active Voice We have learned that you have secured\nthe first rank\n\n(Passive Voice) It has been learned by us that the first\nrank has been secured by you\n\nMORE EXAMPLES\n\n(Active Voice The grandmother was telling ber\ngrandtaliren an interesting story.\n\n(Passive Voice) Am interesting story was being told\nby the grandmother to her grand-\nchildren\nHer grandchildren were being told an\ninteresung story be their grand-\nmother.\n\n(Acuye Voice) ‘The postman brought you @ parcel\n\n(Passive Voice) A parce! was brought to you by a\npostman.\n\nYou were brought a parcel by the\npostman\n\n(Active Voice Thave taught the clacs the first lesson\n\n(Passive Voice) The first deccon was taught by me to\nthe class.\n\nThe class was taught the first lesson\nby me.\n\n(Acuye Voice) ‘The waiter brought us the menu card.\n\n(Passive Voice) ‘The menu card was brought to us by\n\nthe waiter.\n\nWe were brought the menu card by the\nwaiter.\n\nA.280 | Objective English for Competitive Examinations\n\n(Acuye Voice) ‘They are offering you another chance. Atin\n‘A? is used with a definite point of time in\nmind.\nI go to my Department at 7.30. am.\neveryday.\nHe will come at Holt.\n‘In’ is generally used to denote a specific\ntime, period, month, year.\nI play chess in the evening\nI shall come in the next month.\n\nException: ‘At’ is used with noon\nHe comes at noon for lunch.\n\n2. On, by\n‘On’ is used with days and dates.\nHe was bom on the 9th of July.\nI teach Wordsworth on every Monday.\n‘By’ refers to the latest time by which an\naction will be over.\nThe meeting will break by 4 p.m.\n\n3. For, since\n\n‘For denotes a period of time and is used\nwith the perfect continuous tense.\nThave been working in Magadh University\nfor the last 27 years.\n\n‘Since’ shows the point of time. It also\nindicates continuity.\nIndia has been independent since 1947.\n4. From\n\n‘From’ refers to the starting point of an\naction.\nHe is joining the new firm from the Ist of\nMay.\n\n282 | Objective\n\niglish for Compe!\n\nJ Prepositions Showing Position |\n\nAt, in\n\n‘A? refers to an exact point.\nHe lives at Anugrahapun.\n\n‘In’ refers to a big area\nHe lives in Bihar\n\n2. Between, among\n‘Between’ is used to distinguish two persons\nand things\n\n‘The property was divided between Ram\nand Shyam\n‘Among’ is used for more than two persons\nor things\nThe food was distnbuted among the ten\nboys in the family\n\n3. Amongst\n‘Among?’ is also used with more than (wo\npersons or things but is always used before\na vowel\n\nDine the oranges amongst us\n\n4. Above, below\n\n‘Above’ is used for higher than\nThe aeroplane was flying high in the sky, in\n\nfact, above the clouds.\n\n‘Below’ is used for lower than\nHis output is below ours\n\n. Under, over\n\n‘Under’ is used for vertically below.\nWe sit under the tree when we have no\nclass\n\n‘Over indicates something vertically above\nThere 1s a separate room over the\ngarage\n\n. Beneath\n\n‘Beneath’ shows a lower position\nThe ground was sotled beneath her\n\n¢ Examinations\n\nPrepositions Showing\nDirection\n\n1\n\n13.\n\n14.\n\nTo1s used to indicate movement from one place\nto another\n‘The children go fo the school every\nmorning\n\nTowards pounts out a particular direction\nThe Laon ran towards the hunter.\n\n. Info indicates a movement inside something.\n\nThe thief entered sf the room.\n\n|. At indicates aim.\n\nThe hunter aimed af the bird\n\n. For denotes directon\n\nI shall start for Patna today\n\n. Off refers to separation\n\nHe was thrown off from the car during the\naccident\n\n. From refers to a point of departure\n\nWe feel unhappy when we depart from our\nparents.\n\n|. Against shows pressure.\n\nI rested my arms against the wall\n\n. Along shows the same line\n\nI walked along the shore\n\n}. Across means from one side.\n\nT ran across the road.\n\n. Before denotes face-to-face.\n\nIwas standing before my wife.\n\nBehind means at the back of someone or\n\nsomething\nMy daughter stood bebend me\n\nBeade means by the side of\nThe secunty guard sits
