Wat for\nways of\n. wide of\n. wish for\n. watness of\n\n. wonder af\n\nNAP ewe\n\n. write in/ wath\n\nWords Taking More than One\nPreposition\n\nA large number of words are always followed by a\nfixed preposition, for example, insist on; prevent\nfrom; fond of. But certain words take several\nprepositions according to the changing meanings\nof the word. Here is a select list of such words.\n1. Accompany:\nA. By (for living being).\n‘The Pame Minister was accompanied by\nthe members of his Cabinet.\nB. With (subde things).\nHis lecture was accompamed with subde\nanalysis of concepts.\n2. Accountable:\nA. To (an authonty or a person)\nAll of us are accountable to God.\nB. For (action).\nWe are accountable to God for our deeds\nand misdeeds.\n\n3. Angry:\nA. At (a thing), Ram is angry at Shyam’s bad\nconduct.\n\nB. With (a person). Ram is angry with Shyam\nC. For (for a cause). He ts angry with me for\n\nmy remark.\n4. Annoyed:\nA. With (a person). He is annoyed with his\nyounger brother.\n\nB. Af (something). He 1s annoyed with his\nfnend at his laziness.\n\nTheory | A.287\n\n5. Answerable:\nA. To(a person). The servantis answerable to\nthe master.\nB. For (something). We are answerable to our\nparents for our conduct.\n6. Appeak\nA. To (person). He appealed to the judge for\nhis release from jail.\nB. For (thing). He appealed for his release\nfrom the prison.\n7. Arrive:\nA. Af (small place). He arnved at the railway\ncolony.\nB._ In (big place). He arnved in India\n8. Attend:\nA. To (duues, lessons, work). I attend to my\ndunes sincerely.\nB. On (service). Children attend on parents.\n9. Authority:\nA. On (subject). Me. Suresh is an authonty on\nmodern physics.\nB. Over (nghts). The Distnct Magistrate has\nauthonty over the Committee.\n10. Blind:\nA. Of (Physical). He is blind of one eye\nB. To (mental). Many parents are blind to the\nfaults of their children\n11. Communicate:\nA. With (to make correspondence). He\ncommunxated with us on that matter.\nB. To (to convey). He communicated his\nopinion to me.\n12. Compare:\nA. With (Similar things). Shakespeare is\ncompared with Kalidasa\nB. To (dissimilar things). Life is compared to\na battle.\n13. Compete:\nA. With (person). I cannot compete with the\nyoung man.\n\nB._ For(a thing). They compete for a prize\n\n14. Complain:\nA. To (person). He complained to the\nMagistrate against Shyam.\nB. Against (a person). He complained to the\nMagistrate against Shyam.\n©. About (a thing). He complained to the\nMagistrate against Shyam about his\nmisconduct.\n15. Confer:\nA. On (to give). The President conferred the\nude of Bharat Ratna on him.\nB. With (a person). I shall confer with him\nabout important topics.\n16. Die:\nA. Of (a disease). He died of cholera.\nB. From (a cause). He died from overwork.\n17. Differ:\nA. With (person). | differ with you.\nB. From (things). India differs from Japan.\n18. Disqualified:\nA. From (action). He was disqualified from\nrunning the race.\nB. For (post or thing). He was disqualified for\nthe post of Police Inspector.\n19. Entrust:\nA. With (in case of a person). I cannot entrust\nhim with my money.\nB. To (in case of thing). I cannot entrust my\nmoney to him.\n\n20. Familiar:\nA. With (person). | am familiar with the Chief,\nMinister.\nB. To (subject). I am familiar to French\nLanguage.\n21. Fighe\n\nA. With (person). We fought with the English.\nB. For(thing). We fought with the English for\nfreedom.\n22. Gratefuk\nA. To (person). I am grateful to Ram.\nB. For (thing). I am grateful to Ram for his\nhelp.\n\n24.\n\n26.\n\n288 | Objective\n23.\n\nHeir:\n\nA. Of (descendant): A son is usually the heir\nof the father\n\nB._ For (thing). He was heir to a big fortune\n\nIndebted:\n\nA. To(a person). I am indebted to my fend\n\nB._ For (thing). | am indebted to my fnend for\nhis help\n\n. Indignant:\n\nA. With (person). He was indignant with his\nfnends\n\nB.A (thing). The teacher was indignant at his\ncarelessness.\n\nLive:\n\nA. On (to exist). A man lives on food.\n\nB._By (to depend on, to believe in). A terronst\nives by the gun.\n\n. Part:\n\nA. From (persons). He parted from his fnends.\nB. With (things), He cannot part with his\nmoney.\n\n. Responsible:\n\nA. To (person). He was responsible to the\nboss.\n\nB._ For (thing). We are responsible to God for\nour actions\n\n). Taste:\n\nA. Of(expenence). Now you will have a taste\nof new work\nFor (interest). I have no taste for painung\nred.\nA. Of(mental). | am tred of poverty.\nB. With (physical). I am ured with working\nfor five hours continuously.\n\nPractice Set\n\nDirec!\n\njon: Choose the most appropriate preposition:\n\n1. (b)\n\n(a2)\nN\n[a\nLW\nE\na\n<=\n-\nO\n\nAnalogy means similanty or resemblance or some kind\nof relationship between two given things. Analogy\ntest, therefore, intends to evaluate one’s ability to\ncomprehend the relanonship that exists between two\nobjects, things or figures. There are infinite possibilines\nin establishing a relatonship between two objects\nHowever, for our convenience we can chart out some\nimportant kinds of relanonships.\n1. Cause and effect relatonshp\n\nExample: Education : Development\n\n(a) Man: speech\n\n(b) Nutnnon : health\n\n(©) Game : play\n\n(d) Child : growth.\n\nThe relauonship between development and\n\neducation 1s of cause and effect. Educauon.\n\n1s a cause for development. Among the four\n\nchoices only (b) nutrition is a cause for health and\n\nthus this is the answer.\n\n2. Part and whole relationship\n\nExample: House » Room\n\n(a) Struggle : fight (6) Transport: car\n\n(c) School : college(d) Boy: gurl.\n\nRoom is a part of house. Similarly car is a part\nof transport system.\n\n. Part. Part relationship\n\nExample: Gill: Fin\n\n(a) Salad: nce (b) Sea: fish\n\n(©) Kill:bomb (4) Question: team\n\nJust as ge// and fim are two different parts of a\nfish so salad and nce are parts of food.\n\n. Purpose relationship\n\nExample: Glove: Ball\n\n(a) Summer: winter(b) Game : study\n\n(©) Stadium : seats (d) Hook : fish\n\nJust as a gre helps catch a ball, so a book helps\ncatch a fick\n\n. Action to object relationship\n\nExample: Break : Piece\n\n(a) Water: pen\n\n(b) Bread : bake\n\n(©) Kick: football\n\n(d) Muddy : unclear\n\nJust as break is an acton that makes preces, 30\n‘kick is an action that makes the football move.\n\nA.296 | Objective English for Competitive Examinations\n\n6. Worker and tool relanonship\nExample: Sculptor: Chisel\n(a) Pathology : disease\n(b) Tailor : needle\n(c) Engineer: site\n\n(d) Time : seconds.\nJust as a suptor works with a chisel soa tailor\nworks with a needée.\n\n7. Study and topic relanonship\nExample: Langsasties : Language\n(a) Gardener : harrow\n(b) Hen: chicken\n(©) Scale: length\n(d) Anthropology : man\nJust as dngustics 18 the science of language, so\nanthropology 1s the study of man's life\n\n~\n\nWorker and working place relationship\nExample: Lasyer Court\n(a) Professor: university\n(b) Author : book\n(c) Wine: grapes\n(d) Volume : litre\nJust as a layer works at a court so a professor\nworks in a wmveraty\n9. Worker and product relatonship\nExample: Fanner | Crop\n(a) Fabne : yarn\n(b) Cat: kitten\n(c) Wish : desire\n(d) Editor : newspaper\nJust as cps are the products of a fanner’s work\nso a newspaper is the product of an editor's work.\n10. Product and raw matenal relatonship\nExample: Egg: Omellete\n(a) Man: child (b) Spoon : feed\n(©) Judge justice (d) Sack : jute\nJust as an egg is the raw matenal for omelette, so\nputers the raw matenal for a sack\n\nWord and synonym relanonship\nExample: Abate : Lessen\n(a) Sweet: bitter\n\n(b) Ice : sold\n\n(©) Dog: bitch\n(d) Secret: clandestune\nJust as abate and Jessen have similar meanings, so\nseoret or clandestine have similar meanings.\n12. Word and antonym relanonship\nExample: Confidence : diffidence\n(a) Dastard : coward\n(b) Field : farm\n(©) House : garbage\n(d) Baffte : clanfy\nJust as diffidence 1s the opposite of confidence, so\nclarify is the opposite of baffle.\n\n13. Degree relanonship\nExample: Wam - Hot\n(a) Tailor: textile\n(b) Sun: planet\n(c) Hornble : heineous\n(d) Fight: war\nJust as bot is the greater degree of warm, so war\n1s the greater degree of fight\n14. Sequence relationship\nExample: Monday - Tuesday\n(a) Spnng: winter\n(b) Monday : Sunday\n(c) Wednesday : Thursday\n(d) Angle : sky\nJust as Tuecday follows Monday, so Thursday\nfollows Wednesday.\n15. Grammatical relatonship\nExample: He : his\n(a) I:me (b) He: him\n(c) They: these (d) She : her\nJust as be 1s third person nominative and hes\n1s third person possessive case so she is third\nperson nominative case and er is third person\npossessive case.\n16. Sex relanonship\nExample: Lion : lioness.\n(a) Convict: pnson(b) Duck : drake\n(©) Fag:nanon (d) Tiger: leopard\nJust as Zonessis the female of Zon, so drake is the\nfemale of duck\n\nTheory | A.297\n\n17, Object and creature symbol relanonship 1. Thus, in order to have a native’s\ncommand over English, it 1s necessary to understand\nidioms. The Oxford Dictionary of Current Idiomatic English\ndefines an idiom by calling it as a combination of two\nor more words which function as a umt of meaning\nas opposed to non-idiomatic expressions which “are\nmade up of distinct meaningful parts”. Thus, often,\nthe unit of meaning 1s different from what the words\nactually suggest. For example, the expression ‘blue\nblood’ does not mean that blood 1s blue, but it means\n‘to belong to a royal family’. Similarly, the ‘apple of\ndiscord’ does not imply any real apple; it means ‘a cause\nof quarrel’ or ‘Pandora's box’ means a cause of several\nproblems and does not imply any real box though 1t has\nan association with the box of Pandora. The expenence\ncomes out in an idiom in a standardised form.\n\n‘The test 1s usually set in two forms; either in direct\nmuluple choice or in a sentence form.\n\nA. (©) 13. @) 14. ©) 1s @)\n16. @) 17. @) 18. &) 19. &) 20. @)\n21. @) 22. () 23. ©) 24. 0) 28. ©)\n\nuy\nN\n[a\nLW\nE\na\n<=\na\nO\n\nNarranon 1s the art of reporting the words of a speaker.\nThere are two main ways of reportng the words of a speaker.\n1. Direct Speech\n2. Indirect Speech\n\npDiret Speech\n\nIn this form the actual words of the speaker are putin\nquotes “00.”\nExample\nRant said,” I am very busy now.”\nIn the above example the speaker, .¢,\nRant 1s known as reporter, ‘said’ 1s known as\nreporting verb and\n“Lam very busy now” 1s known as reported\nspeech.\n\n[indirect Speech\n\nIn this form the actual words of the speaker are\ntransformed and saxd/wntten in simple manner.\nExample: Rani said that she was very busy then.\nBasic rules to convert a Direct Speech to Indirect\nSpeech\n\nThere are five basic things that have to be changed\nwhile converting a Direct Speech to an indirect\nspeech\n1. To remove the inverted commas from\nthe direct speech and place them with an\nappropnate conjunction\n2. To change the pronoun of reported speech\naccordingly\n3. To change the reporting verb according to\nthe reported speech\n4. Change the adverbs of the direct speech\nDerect- Rajnish said to me,” I shall go to for\nthe movie today”.\nInderect Raynish told me that he would go for the movie\nthat day.\nIn the above example:\nReporting verb ‘said to’ 1s changed into ‘told’.\nInverted commas are replaced by the conjunction\nthat\nReported speechs pronoun ‘I’ is changed into ‘he’.\nReported speech’s verb ‘shall go’ 1s changed into\n‘would go’\nThe word ‘today’ 1s changed with the word\n‘that day’\n\n320 | Objective English for Compe\n\nJPules of Change of Pronouns |\nNominative | Possessive | Objective | Reflexive |\n' [ My | Mo za\na ee\na\na |\na |\nes ee |\n\nChanges in pronoun in Indirect Speech\n‘The pronoun (subject) of the reported speech is\nchanged according to the pronoun of reporting verb\nor object (person) of reporting verb (first part of\nsentence), Sometimes the pronoun may not change\nat all\n1. First person pronoun in reported speech 1. 1,\nwe, me, us, mine, or our, is changed according\nto the pronoun of reporting verb if pronoun in\nreporting verb is third person pronoun, 1¢., he,\nshe, it, they, him, his, her, them or their.\n\nt EXAMPLES\n\nDirect speech: Sonu said, “I live in New Delhi”\nIndirect speech: Sonu said that he lived in New Delhi\n\nDirect speech: They said, “We love our country”\nIndirect speech: They said that they loved their\ncountry.\n2. If the first part of sentence (reporting verb\npart) belongs to present or future tense, the tense of\nreported speech will not change\n\nDirect speech: Nechma said, “I am happy’\n\nIndirect Speech: Neclima said that she was happy\n(Tense of reported speech changed)\nDirect speech: Nechma says, “I am happy”\nIndirect Speech: Nechma says that she was happy\n(Tense of reported speech didn’t sentences\n\nIndirect speech of imperative sentence\n\nA sentence which expresses command, request, advice\nor suggestion is called imperative sentence.\n\nExamples\n* Open the door\n\n* Please help me\n\n* Leam your lesson.\n\nTo change such sentences into indirect speech,\nthe word “ordered” or “requested” or “advised”\nor “suggested” or “forbade” or “not to do” 1s\nadded to reporting verb depending upon the\nnature of the imperative sentence in reported\nspeech\n\nEXAMPLES\n\nDirect Speech: Anish said to me, “Please help me”\nIndirect Speech: Anish requested me to help him\nDirect Without a vehicle, divers often\nbecame sluggish, and their mental concentration was\nseverely limited. Because undersea pressure affects\ntheir speech organs, communication among divers has\nalways been difficult or impossible. But today, most\noceanographers avoid the use of vulnerable human\ndivers, preferring to reduce the nsk to human life and\nmake direct observations by means of instruments that\nare lowered into the ocean, from samples taken from the\nwater, or from photographs made by orbiting satellites.\nDirect observanons of the ocean floor can be made\nnot only by divers but also by deepdiving submannes in\nthe water and even by the technology of sophisticated\nacnal photography from vantage points above the\nsurface of the water. Some submannes can dive to\ndepths of more than seven miles and cruise at depths\nof fifteen thousand feet. In addinon, radio-cquipped\nbuoys can be operated by remote control in order to\n\ntransmit information back to land-based laboratones\nvia satellite. Particularly umportant for ocean study are\ndata about water temperature, currents, and weather\nSatellite photographs can show the distnbution of sea\nace, of slicks, and cloud formations over the ocean\nMaps created from satellite pictures can represent the\ntemperature and die colour of the ocean's surface,\nenabling researchers to study the ocean currents from\nlaboratones on dry land. Furthermore, computers help\noceanographers to collect, organize, and analyze data\nfrom submannes and satellites By creating a model\nof the ocean’s movement and charactenstics, scientists\ncan predict the patterns and possible effects of the\nocean on the environment.\n\nRecently, many oceanographers have been relying\nmore on satellites and computers than on research\nships or even submanne vehicles because they can\nsupply a greater range of information more quickly\nand more effectively. Some of humankind’s most\nserious problems, especially those concerning energy\nand food, may be solved with the help of observanons\nmade possible by this new technology.\n\n1. Certain words/phrases have been printed\nin bold to help you locate them while answering\nsome of the questions.\n\nIt was in the offing With shortages mounting\nacross the board for water as they are for energy, it was\nonly inevitable that the Central government would\nbe stirred into starting a Bureau of Water Efficiency\n(BWE), much hke the Bureau of Energy Efficiency\n(BEF) that was launched some years ago.\n\nFarly reports suggest that the draft norms for\nvarious sectors consuming water will be created by\nthe BWE soon. The alarm bells have been nngang for\nsome years now Water availability per capita in India\nhas fallen from about 5 milhon litres in the 1950s to 1.3\nmillion litres in 2010 -that’s a staggering 75 per cent\ndrop in 50 years. Nearly 60 per cent of India’s aquifers\nhave slumped to cntical levels in just the last-15 years\nThe rate at which borewells are being plunged in every\ncity with no law to ban such extraction, groundwater\ntables have depleted alarmingly.\n\nThe BEE's efforts in the last seven years have only\nbeen cosmetic. The bureau has looked at efficiency\nrating systems for white goods in the domestic sector,\n\nand has not paid attention to the massive consumption\nof energy in metals manufacture, paper, and textiles\nThese sectors are very intense in both energy and water\nconsumption. But very hittle attention has been paid to\nthe water and energy used per tonne of steel or cement\nor aluminium that we buy, and without significant\nchanges in these areas, the overall situation 1s unhkely\nto change.\n\nUse of water ts inextricably interlinked with\nenergy One does not exist without the other. The\nBWE should steer clear of the carly mistakes of BEF.\n- of focusing on the “softer targets’ in the domesne\nsector. Nearly 80 per cent of fresh water is used by\nagneulture, with industry coming a close second. The\ndomesnc sector's consumption of fresh water 1s in\nsingle digit. So the BWE?s pnonty should be to look\nat measures that will gel farmers and industnalists to\nfollow good practices in water use. Certain words/phrases have been printed\nin bold to help you locate them while answering\nsome of the question.\nHealth insurance policies were first introduced in\n1986 at a ime when the Indian insurance industry was\nnationalised. The policies on offer were complicated\nto read and offered limited cover. There were no third\nparty administrators operating in India, and there was\nno direct settlement of claims between health insurer\nand hospital There were therefore issues conceming\nclams servicing, which involved an Insured following\ncumbersome procedures to get claims authenticated\nand paid. The business was not profitable for the\nnationalised insurers, and not popular wath the public\nat large\n\nThe onginal ‘Mediclam Policy’, however,\ndeveloped and in many cases has provided the base\nmodel for the health care insurance policies that were\nintroduced immediately after hberalisation of the\ngeneral insurance sector at the tum of the Millennium\n\nHealth insurance, however, saw no specialist\nplayers unnl relatively recently, This is because there\nwas a general expectation that, the insurance industry\n\nregulator, the IRDA would set a smaller capitalisation\nrequirement for health insurers and/or amend the\nrules for foreign equity ownership in Indian Insurers\nin recognition of the fact that health msurance loss\nratios were not good, and therefore finding an Indian\npartner to invest 76% in a health insurer would be a\ndifficult task. The IRDA did not, however, relax either\nthe capitalisation requirements or foreign investments\ncaps. Initially, therefore, the health insurance market\ndid not grow as quickly as may have been expected.\nGrowth in policyholder numbers, more effective\nthird party administration and an effective network of\nhospitals ts expected to see the numbers improve. Other\nchanges have been effected to encourage growth in this\nsector. Life insurers have been allowed to sell health\ninsurance. Initially, life insurers were only allowed to sell\ncertain types of health covers as a supplement to a life\npolicy. However, the IRDA has allowed hfe insurers to\nsell pure health insurance products subject to product\nspecific approvals. The standard mediclaim policy has\nundergone several revisions and modifications. In\nrecent years, private health insurers have been offenng\nfresh products with increased covers and sums insured.\nPrvate hospital rates are still low compared to the rates\ncharged in more developed countnes, but high when\n\ncompared to average Indian camnings. It is no longer\nuncommon for Indian employees to now expect that\nhealth care will be partof an employment package. With\nthe opening up of the market to private competion,\nthe claims process has become much less cumbersome\nSupport for a health insurance market has also come\nfrom some less obvious sources. Indian states have\nstarted relying on insurance policies to meet some of\ntheir legal obliganions to provide health care to their\ncitizens. The central government has also proposed the\nintroduction of free health care insurance for the poor\nThis plan is meant to cover every poor family for INR\n30,000 per annum. The central government will pay\n\n75% of the premium, leaving the remaining 25% to\nbe covered by state governments. The IRDA has also\nencouraged Micro-insurance as a means of extending\nthe availability of health insurance to areas of the\nmarket that, geographically and economically, may not\nhave been at the forefront of Insurers’ business plans\nAt the same time as the market grows, the IRDA 1s\nstepping in to create a more consumer fnendly playing\nfield, particularly as regards the treatment of senior\ncitizens, the operation of the preexisting diseases\nexclusion, and the reluctance of insurers to renew\npolicies where the claims expenence has been bad\nSenior citizens had been complaining about the\nreluctance of Insurers to issue policies to them, and\nthe inclusion of disadvantageous terms when policies\nwere offered—such as hefty increases in premium rates,\nadded exclusions and conditions, etc. In May 2007,\nthe IRDA set up a Committee on Health Insurance\nfor Senior Cinzens to make recommendations. The\nCommuttee reported in November 2007 and made\nthe following main recommendations: Senior Citizens\nshould have some assurance that their pohicies will be\nrenewed; the Industry should adopt standard terms\nand conditions, such as for the defininon of pre-\nexisting diseases. The Commuttee also said that policy\nwordings should be simpler for the lay person to\nfollow, suggesting that uniform terminology be used\nby all Insurers to lessen confusion in the public mind\n\n1, Answer the questions on\nthe basis of what is stated or implied in the passage.\n\nNot long ago there was a discussion in which the\npanelists talked about the ‘future of religion’. Towards\nthe end of this half-hour programme it became\nobvious that the discussants were not going to be able\nto agree on the ‘future of rehgion—whether indeed\nit has a future. Quite properly, one panelist suggested\nthat since apparently the word did not mean the\nsame thing to the various participants, perhaps each\nmember of the panel should state how he was using\nthe term. But at this stage, all their ime had been used\nup, and the thing they should have done at the outset\nwas never even to have begun. Another illustration\nof the complexity of the manifestations of ‘religions’\nand resulting vagueness of meaning of the term ts\nprovided by the story of the graduate student in one\nof our great universites. He ‘earned’ an M, Phil degree\nby cataloguing in his thesis the vanous definitions of\nreligion that appear in literature stopping when he had\nculled fifty defimtons. A cynic might pomt out that\nif this had been a doctoral dissertation, the student\ncould have gone on with the research and collected one\nhundred definitons.\n\nOne might surmise that a major conclusion to.\nresult from an adequate discussion of the’ future of\nreligion would be this: if one employs a narrow and\nngid definition, then relgion in its organised and\ninsitution-alised forms may have little future, but if\none recognises that religion ts a dynamic thing capable\nof flexibilty, it may have a future as bnght as that of\nthe human race—indeed, it might even help light the\nway to this uncertain world of tomorrow.\n\nThe New Century Dictionary defines religion as\nthe ‘recognition’ on the part of man of a controlling,\nsuperhuman power ‘enntled to reverence and\nworship’. If this ‘superhuman power’ 1s not hmited to\na personalised enuty, but 1s broad enough to include\nthe more abstractly conceived realities such as the\nuniversal lawfulness of nature of the pantheists and\nthe ‘infinite etemal energy’ of Herbert Spencer, there\n1s no difficulty in accepting the New Century Dictionary\ndefimiton.\n\nThe importance of the problem of defininon in\ndetermining the character of one’s conclusions was\nbrought out in an interesting manner when the [fe\nmagazne in an article on the Huxley brothers descnbed\nJulian Huxley as ‘an atheist” and ‘a matenalist, denying\nthe need for religion or God’. Commenting on the\nartcle, Dr Charles Francis Potter, founder of the First\n\nB.50 | Objec\n\nHumanist Society of New York, accused the wnter of\n\na non seguttul: Dt Potter agreed that Julian Huxley does\ndeny the need for God, but stated that the assertion\nthat Dr Huxley therefore denies the need for religion 1s\nevidently based on the false assumption that one must\n\nbelieve in God to have a Certain words/phrases\nhave been printed in bold to help you locate them\nwhile answering some of the questions.\nGovernments have traditionally equated economic\nprogress with stecl mills and cement factories.\nWhile urban centres thnve and city dwellers get nch,\nhundreds of milhons of farmers remain mired in\npoverty. However, fears of food shortage, a rethinking\nof antipoverty pnontes and the crushing recession in\n2008 are causing a dramanc shift in world economic\npolicy in favour of greater support, for agnculture.\nThe last ume when the world’s farmers felt such\nlove was In the 1970s. At that ume, as food pnces\nspiked, there was real concern that the world was\nfacing a cnsis in which the planet was simply unable\nto produce enough grain and meat for an expanding\npopulanon. Government across the developing world\nand internabonal aid organisations plowed investment\ninto agnculture in the early 1970s, while technological\nbreakthroughs, like high-yield strains of important\n\nfood crops, boosted production. The result was the\nGreen Revolution and food production exploded\n\nBut the Green Revolution became a victim of.\nits own success. Food prices plunged by some 60%\nby the late 1980s from their peak in the mid-1970s.\nPohcy makers and aid workers tumed their attention\nto the poor’s other pressing needs, such as health\ncare and education. Farming got starved of resources\nand investment. By 2004, aid directed at agnculture\nsank to 3.5% and “Agneculture lost its glitter”. Also, as\nconsumers in high-growth giants such as China and In-\ndia became wealthier, they began eating more meat, so\ngrain once used for human consumption got diverted\nto beef up livestock. By carly 2008, panicked buying\nby importing countnes and restnctions slapped on\ngrain exports by some big producers helped drive\nprices upto heights not seen for three decades: Making\nmatters worse, land and resources got reallocated to\nproduce cash crops such as biofuels and the result was\nthe voluminous reserves of grain evaporated. Protests\nbroke out across the emerging world and fierce food\nnots toppled governments,\n\nThis spurred global leaders into action. This made\nthem aware that food secunty 1s one of the fundamen-\n\nPrevious Years’ Question Papers | C.23\n\ntal issues in the world that has to be dealt with in order\nto maintain admunistranve and polincal stability. This\nalso spurred the US. which traditionally provisioned\nfood aid from Amencan grain surpluses to help needy\nnations, to move towards investing in farm sectors\naround the globe to boost productvity. This move\nhelped countnes become more productive for them-\nselves and be in a better positon to feed their own\npeople.\n\nAfnea, which missed out on the first Green\nRevolution due to poor policy and limited resources,\nalso witnessed a ‘change’. Swayed by the success of\nEast Asia, the pnmary poverty-fighting method\nfavoured by many policymakers in Afnca was to get\nfarmers off their farms and into modern jobs in factors\nand urban centres. But that strategy proved to be highly\ninsufficient. Income levels in the countryside badly\ntrailed those in cines while the FAO esumated that the\nnumber of poor going hungry in 2009 reached an all\ntme high at more than one billion.\n\nIn India on the other hand, with only 40%\nof its farmland irngated, entre economic boom\ncurrently underway is held hostage by the unpredict-\nable monsoon. With much of India’s farming areas\nsuffering from drought this year, the government\nwill have a tough ume meeting its economic growth\ntargets. In a report, Goldman Sachs predicted that\nif this year too receives weak rains, it could cause\nagnculture to contract by 2% this fiscal year, making\nthe government's 7% GDP-growth target look “a bit\nrich”. Another green revolution 1s the need of the\nhour and to make ita realty, the global community sull\nhas much backbreaking farm work to do.\n\n1. Certain words/phrases\nhave been printed in bold to help you locate them\nwhile answering some of the questions.\nGovernments have tradinonally equated economic\nprogress with steel mills and cement factories.\nWhile urban centres thnve and city dwellers get ach,\nhundreds of milhons of farmers remain mired in\npoverty. However, fears of food shortage, a rethinking\nof antipoverty pnonnes and the crushing recession in\n2008 are causing a dramatic shift in world economic\npolicy in favour of greater support for agnculture.\n\nThe last ume when the world’s farmers felt such\nlove was in the 1970s. At that ume, as food pnces\nspiked, there was real concern that the world was\nfacing a cnsis in which the planet was simply unable\nto produce enough grain and meat for an expanding\npopulanon. Government across the developing world\nand internabonal aid organisations plowed investment\ninto agnculture in the early 1970s, while technological\nbreakthroughs, like high-yield strains of important\nfood crops, boosted production. The result was the\n\nGreen Revolution and food production exploded\n\nBut the Green Revolution became a victim of\nits own success. Food prices plunged by some 60%\nby the late 1980s from their peak in the mid-1970s.\nPolicy makers and aid workers tumed their attention\nto the poor’s other pressing needs, such as health\ncare and education. Farming got starved of resources\nand investment. By 2004, aid directed at agnculture\nsank to 3.5% and “Agnculture lost its ghtter”. By early 2008, panicked\nbuying by importing countnes and restnctions slapped\non grain exports by some big producers helped drive\nprices upto heights not seen for three decades: Making\nmatters worse, land and resources got reallocated to\nproduce cash crops such as biofuels and the result was\nthe voluminous reserves of grain evaporated. Science should be taught not merely\nas a subject, but should come into all subjects. Its\nimportance in history and in modem hfe should be\npointed out and illustrated. The old contrast, often\namounting to hostility, between scientific and humane\nsubjects needs to be broken down and replaced by a\nscientific humanism. At the same time, the teaching\nof science requires to be humanised. The dry and\nfactual presentation requires to be transformed, not\nby an appeal to mystical theory, but by emphasising\nthe living and dramanc character of scientific advance\nitself. Here the teaching of the history of science, not\nisolated as at present, but in close relation to general\nhistory teaching, would serve to correct the existing\natmosphere of scientific dogmansm. It would show at\nthe same time how secure are the conquests of science\nin the control they give over natural processes and how\n\ninsecure and provisional, however necessary, are the\nrational interpretations, the theones and hypotheses\nput forward at each stage Past history by itself is not\nenough, the latest development of science should not\nbe excluded because they have not yet passed the test\nof time. Its absolutely necessary to emphasise the fact\nthat science not only has changed but is continually\nchanging, that it ts an activity and not merely a body of\nfacts. Throughout, the social implications of science,\nthe powers that it puts into men’s hands, the uses they\ncould make of them and those which they in fact do,\nshould be brought out and made real by a reference to\nimmediate expenence of ordinary life\n\n21. India embraced\nthis vision at its independence. However, insufficient\nfunding of public facilines, combined with faulty\nplanning and inefficient management over the y\nhas resulted in a dysfunctional health system that has\nbeen yielding poor health outcomes. Private health\n\nars,\n\nservices have grown by default, without checks on cost\nand quality, escalating pnvate out-of-pocket health\nexpenditures and exacerbaung health inequity. Out-\nof-pocket expenditure still remains at 71 percent of\nall spending, without coverage for outpatient care,\nmedicines and basic diagnostic tests. ‘The High Level\nExpert Group (HLEG) established by the Planning\nCommission has submitted acomprehensive framework\nfor providing UHC in India. A health enutlement card\nshould assure every cinzen access to a national health\npackage of essental pnmary, secondary and ternary\ncare, both inpatient and outpatient. The HLEG is very\nclear that services included under UHC must be tax\nfunded and cashless at delivery. Contnbutory social\ninsurance 1s not appropnate for countnes hike India\nwhere a large segment of the workforce —close to 93\npercent—is in the unorganised sector and vast numbers\nare below or near the poverty line. Increasing the public\nspending on health 1s the first immediate requirement\nHowever, even the doubling of public financing will\nnot be adequate to support all the components of\na fully evolved UHC. Pnonnes need to be defined\nClearly, there is no alternative to progressively\nstrengthening public facilites, and thereby reducing\npeople’s dependence on pnvate providers. However,\nthe public system may need to “contract-in” the\nservices of willing private providers, to fill gaps in its\ncapacity to deliver all the services assured under UHC.\nSuch “contracted-in” private providers will have to\ndeliver cashless services and would be compensated on\nthe basis of pre-determined cost per package of health\nservices rather than “fee-for-service” for each visit or\nprocedure. In such an arrangement, the pavate sector\nacts as an extension of the public, sector where needed,\nand will not compete for the same set of services for\nthe same people. It is ume to recognise that everyone,\nnot just the poor, needs to be protected against nsing\nhealth costs that can impovensh any family. Apart from\nimproving people’s health, adopung UHC is likely to\ngenerate millions of new jobs, enhance producuuity,\nand promote equity.\n\n14. India spans longitudes of 68° at the western\nend and 98° at the eastern boundary and, as there 1s a\ndifference of one hour for every 15° of longitude, the\ntwo extremes differ by two hours Thus, when the sun\nsets at 4 p.m. in Kohima, it sets at 6 p.m. in Porbunder.\nIST was fixed in 1906 midway at 82.5°, or 5.5 hours\nahead of Greenwich Mean Time (GMT). There is also the practice in several countnes,\nof “Dayhght Saving Time” (DST), wherein the ume\nin summer is advanced (or the clocks put forward) by\none hour and retracted dunng winter. This enables\npeople to enjoy sunlight longer in summer and avoid\nthe inconveniences of late sunnses and early sunsets\ndunng winter. Our proposal for India is to introduce\nneither ume zones nor DST, but to advance IST by half\nan hour to being six hours ahead of GMT, once and\npermanently. In the first seven years the child’s\nbrain 1s like a sponge, taking in all sensory mputs and\nbuilding his idea of his surroundings As long as the\nenvironment is safe, the child learns with incredible\nspeed. However, when the environment ts scary or\nstressful, the child unleams past learning just as rapidly.\n\nIn the early years of every child’s life, whenever\nthere 1s shock, violence, fear or pain, these intense\nemotions are impnated deeply into memory. Whenever\n\nthe same activity or situation is repeated, the nervous\nsystem and body subconsciously re-experience the\nmemory of that trauma\n\nAny emotional situauon that takes us out of the\npresent and into the past means that whenever the same\nkind of emotion crops up later in out-life we return to\nthe past for our reference point. If that point was at age\nthree, we find ourselves behaving like a three-year-old\nWe feel childish and we behave childishly. Our feelings\nare the cause of this ‘ghtch’ in our learning process. We\nknow we should be able to make a positive change, but\nthat doesn’t change anything\n\n‘The process of change need not be traumatic. We\ncouldn't have done any better because we didn’t know\nhow to. But we should realise that was then and this is\nnow! We can choose to choose again. It’s up to us. It’s\nour movie!\n\n38. Nos. 191 to 200)\n\nThe habit of reading 1s one of the greatest resources\nof mankind; and we enjoy reading books that belong to\nus much more than if they are borrowed. A borrowed\nbook ts hke a guest in the house; it must be treated with\npunctiliousness, with certain considerate formality. You\nmust sce that it sustains no damage, it must not suffer\nwhile under your roof. You cannot leave it carelessly,\nyou cannot mark it, you cannot turn down the pages,\nyou cannot use it famiharly.\n\nBut your own books belong to you; you treat\nthem with that affectionate intimacy that annihilates\nformality.\n\nBooks are for use, not for show. A good reason for\nmarking favounte pages in books 1s that this pracnce\nenables you to remember more easily the significant\nsayings, to refer to them quickly.\n\nC.294 | Objective\n\nEveryone should begin collecting a pnvate library\nin youth; one should have one’s own bookshelves,\nwhich should not have doors, glass windows, or keys,\nthey should be free and accessible to the hand as well as\nto the eye. Nos. 191 to 200)\n\nArt both reflects and interprets the notion that\nproduced it. Portraiture was the dominant theme of\nBrush painung up to the end of the eighteenth century\nbecause of a persistent demand forit. Itwould be unfar\nto say that human vanity and pnde of possessions\nwere the only reasons for this persistent demand, but\ncertainly these motives played their part in shaping the\ncourse of Bnush painung Generally speaking, itis the\nartst’s enthusiasm that accounts for the vitality of the\npicture, but it is the chent who dictates its subject mat-\nter. The history of natonal enthusiasms can be pretty\naccurately esumated by examining the subject matter\nof anaton’s art.\n\nThere is one type of subject which recurs again\nand again in Bnush painung of the late eighteenth\ncentury and the first half of the nineteenth and which\n\naminations\n\nis hardly met with in the part of any other country —\nthe sporting picture, or rather the picture in which a\nlove of outdoor hfe 1s directed into the channel of\nsport. The sporting picture 1s really an extension of\nthe conversation piece. In it the emphasis is even more\nfirmly based on the descriptive side of painting It made\nsevere demands on the artist and it must be confessed\nthat painters capable of satisfying these demands\nwere rare. The ability to paint a reasonably convincing\nlandscape is not often combined with the necessary\nknowledge of horses and dogs in) movement and\nthe power to introduce a portrait when necessary. To\nweld such diverse elements into a satisfactory aesthetic\nunity requires exceptional ability. It 1s not surpnsing,\ntherefore, that while sporting pictures abound in\nEngland, especially in the private collections of country\nsquires, not many of them are of real importance as\nworks of art. What makes the sporting picture worth\nnotng, a history of Bnush painng is the fact that itis\nas truly indigenous and as truly popular a form of art\nin England as was the religious ikon in Russia.\n\n191.
